siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.name: Nucleolus Organization
siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.description: A process that is carried out at the cellular level which results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of the nucleolus.

siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.name: Cajal Body Organization
siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.description: A process that is carried out at the cellular level which results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of Cajal bodies.

siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.name: Nuclear Speckle Organization
siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.description: A process that is carried out at the cellular level which results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of nuclear speckles.

siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.name: PML Body Organization
siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.description: A process that results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of Promyelocytic Leukemia (PML) nuclear bodies.

siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.name: Paraspeckle Organization
siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.description: A process that results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of paraspeckles within the nucleus.

siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.name: Histone Locus Body Organization
siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.description: A process that results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of histone locus bodies.

siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.name: Perinucleolar Compartment Organization
siblings:subnuclear structure organizati.description: A process that results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of the perinucleolar compartment.

siblings:intracellular transport.name: Vesicle-Mediated Transport
siblings:intracellular transport.description: The movement of proteins and lipids between organelles via membrane-bound carriers.

siblings:intracellular transport.name: Nucleocytoplasmic Transport
siblings:intracellular transport.description: The regulated exchange of proteins, RNA, and ribonucleoprotein complexes between the nucleus and the cytoplasm.

siblings:intracellular transport.name: Endosomal Transport
siblings:intracellular transport.description: The sorting and trafficking of internalized molecules through the endocytic pathway to various cellular destinations.

siblings:intracellular transport.name: Retrograde Transport
siblings:intracellular transport.description: The movement of proteins and membranes from the Golgi apparatus back to the endoplasmic reticulum.

siblings:intracellular transport.name: Protein Folding and Export from ER
siblings:intracellular transport.description: The quality control process and subsequent exit of correctly folded proteins from the endoplasmic reticulum.

siblings:intracellular transport.name: Lysosomal Transport
siblings:intracellular transport.description: The targeted delivery of hydrolytic enzymes and materials for degradation to the lysosome.

siblings:intracellular transport.name: Mitochondrial Protein Import
siblings:intracellular transport.description: The translocation of nuclear-encoded proteins across the mitochondrial membranes.

siblings:autophagy.name: Microautophagy
siblings:autophagy.description: The direct engulfment of cytoplasmic cargo by the lysosomal or vacuolar membrane via inward invagination.

siblings:autophagy.name: Chaperone-mediated autophagy
siblings:autophagy.description: A selective pathway where proteins containing a KFERQ-like motif are translocated across the lysosomal membrane via hsc70 and LAMP-2A.

siblings:autophagy.name: Mitophagy
siblings:autophagy.description: The selective degradation of mitochondria by autophagy in response to damage or metabolic cues.

siblings:autophagy.name: Pexophagy
siblings:autophagy.description: The selective autophagic degradation of peroxisomes to maintain cellular homeostasis.

siblings:autophagy.name: Xenophagy
siblings:autophagy.description: The autophagic process of capturing and degrading intracellular pathogens such as bacteria and viruses.

siblings:autophagy.name: Lipophagy
siblings:autophagy.description: The selective degradation of lipid droplets via the autophagic machinery to mobilize stored fatty acids.

siblings:autophagy.name: Ribophagy
siblings:autophagy.description: The selective degradation of ribosomes, often triggered by nutrient starvation to recycle amino acids.

siblings:gene expression.name: Transcription
siblings:gene expression.description: The process by which the information in a DNA sequence is copied into a new molecule of messenger RNA.

siblings:gene expression.name: RNA Processing
siblings:gene expression.description: The modification of the primary RNA transcript into a mature mRNA molecule, including splicing and polyadenylation.

siblings:gene expression.name: Translation
siblings:gene expression.description: The synthesis of a polypeptide chain by ribosomes using the genetic information carried by mRNA.

siblings:gene expression.name: Chromatin Organization
siblings:gene expression.description: The structural modification of chromatin, such as histone modification and DNA methylation, to regulate gene accessibility.

siblings:gene expression.name: mRNA Decay
siblings:gene expression.description: The regulated degradation of mRNA molecules which determines the stability and availability of transcripts for translation.

siblings:gene expression.name: Ribosome Biogenesis
siblings:gene expression.description: The process of manufacturing ribosomes, the cellular machinery required for translating genetic information into proteins.

siblings:intercellular signaling.name: Endocrine Signaling
siblings:intercellular signaling.description: Communication using hormones released into the bloodstream to reach distant target cells.

siblings:intercellular signaling.name: Paracrine Signaling
siblings:intercellular signaling.description: Local communication where signaling molecules affect neighboring cells within the same tissue.

siblings:intercellular signaling.name: Autocrine Signaling
siblings:intercellular signaling.description: A process where a cell secretes molecules that bind to receptors on its own surface.

siblings:intercellular signaling.name: Juxtacrine Signaling
siblings:intercellular signaling.description: Contact-dependent signaling requiring physical interaction between membrane-bound molecules of adjacent cells.

siblings:intercellular signaling.name: Synaptic Signaling
siblings:intercellular signaling.description: Fast intercellular communication occurring across specialized junctions called synapses in the nervous system.

siblings:intercellular signaling.name: Gap Junction Signaling
siblings:intercellular signaling.description: Direct transmission of ions and small molecules through protein channels connecting the cytoplasm of adjacent cells.

siblings:programmed cell death signaling.name: Necroptosis
siblings:programmed cell death signaling.description: A regulated form of necrotic cell death mediated by RIPK1, RIPK3, and MLKL proteins.

siblings:programmed cell death signaling.name: Pyroptosis
siblings:programmed cell death signaling.description: A highly inflammatory form of programmed cell death often triggered by inflammasome activation.

siblings:programmed cell death signaling.name: Ferroptosis
siblings:programmed cell death signaling.description: An iron-dependent form of regulated cell death characterized by the accumulation of lipid peroxides.

siblings:programmed cell death signaling.name: Autophagy-dependent cell death
siblings:programmed cell death signaling.description: A form of regulated cell death that mechanistically depends upon the autophagic machinery.

siblings:programmed cell death signaling.name: Parthanatos
siblings:programmed cell death signaling.description: A unique programmed cell death pathway characterized by overactivation of PARP-1 and translocation of AIF.

siblings:programmed cell death signaling.name: NETosis
siblings:programmed cell death signaling.description: A specialized form of programmed cell death in neutrophils involving the release of extracellular traps.

siblings:programmed cell death signaling.name: Entosis
siblings:programmed cell death signaling.description: A non-apoptotic cell death process involving the engulfment of one cell by another.

siblings:dna damage response.name: Cell Cycle Checkpoints
siblings:dna damage response.description: Mechanisms that halt cell cycle progression to allow time for DNA repair or to prevent the replication of damaged templates.

siblings:dna damage response.name: DNA Damage Signaling
siblings:dna damage response.description: Transduction cascades, primarily mediated by ATM, ATR, and DNA-PK kinases, that coordinate cellular responses to genotoxic stress.

siblings:dna damage response.name: Apoptosis
siblings:dna damage response.description: Programmed cell death pathways triggered when DNA damage is too severe to be repaired, ensuring the elimination of potentially oncogenic cells.

siblings:dna damage response.name: Cellular Senescence
siblings:dna damage response.description: A state of permanent cell cycle arrest that acts as a tumor suppressive mechanism in response to persistent DNA damage.

siblings:dna damage response.name: Transcriptional Response
siblings:dna damage response.description: Global changes in gene expression patterns to prioritize the synthesis of proteins required for genome maintenance and stress survival.

siblings:dna damage response.name: Chromatin Remodeling
siblings:dna damage response.description: Modification of chromatin architecture to increase the accessibility of DNA lesions to signaling and repair machinery.

siblings:axonal transport.name: Anterograde Axonal Transport
siblings:axonal transport.description: The movement of organelles and molecules from the cell body toward the distal axon and synapse.

siblings:axonal transport.name: Slow Axonal Transport
siblings:axonal transport.description: The transport mechanism for cytoskeletal components and cytosolic proteins, characterized by significantly lower speeds than organelle transport.

siblings:axonal transport.name: Fast Anterograde Transport
siblings:axonal transport.description: Kinesin-mediated rapid movement of membrane-bound organelles, vesicles, and neurotransmitter precursors toward the axon terminal.

siblings:axonal transport.name: Mitochondrial Axonal Transport
siblings:axonal transport.description: The regulated bidirectional movement of mitochondria to satisfy local energy and calcium buffering demands along the axon.

siblings:axonal transport.name: Slow Component a (SCa)
siblings:axonal transport.description: A subcategory of slow transport primarily responsible for moving microtubules and neurofilaments.

siblings:axonal transport.name: Slow Component b (SCb)
siblings:axonal transport.description: A subcategory of slow transport that conveys over 200 diverse proteins, including actin, clathrin, and metabolic enzymes.

siblings:axonal transport.name: Autophagosome Axonal Transport
siblings:axonal transport.description: The specific movement of autophagic vacuoles from distal axons to the soma for degradation and recycling.

siblings:cell fate regulation.name: Cell Proliferation
siblings:cell fate regulation.description: The process by which cells increase in number through growth and division.

siblings:cell fate regulation.name: Cell Differentiation
siblings:cell fate regulation.description: The developmental process where cells acquire specialized structures and functions.

siblings:cell fate regulation.name: Cell Senescence
siblings:cell fate regulation.description: A stable state of permanent cell cycle arrest in response to cellular stress.

siblings:cell fate regulation.name: Cell Quiescence
siblings:cell fate regulation.description: A reversible state of non-proliferation where cells remain in the G0 phase.

siblings:cell fate regulation.name: Cell Migration
siblings:cell fate regulation.description: The coordinated movement of cells to specific locations to establish tissue patterns.

siblings:cell fate regulation.name: Autophagy
siblings:cell fate regulation.description: A conserved degradation process that recycles cellular components to maintain homeostasis.

siblings:protein quality control.name: Cytosolic Protein Quality Control
siblings:protein quality control.description: The process of monitoring and maintaining the integrity of the proteome within the cytoplasm through chaperones and the ubiquitin-proteasome system.

siblings:protein quality control.name: Mitochondrial Protein Quality Control
siblings:protein quality control.description: A network of specialized chaperones and proteases that ensures the functional integrity and folding of proteins within the mitochondria.

siblings:protein quality control.name: Nucleoplasmic Protein Quality Control
siblings:protein quality control.description: The mechanism responsible for the detection and degradation of misfolded or aggregated proteins located within the cell nucleus.

siblings:protein quality control.name: Ribosome-associated Quality Control
siblings:protein quality control.description: A pathway that monitors translation to detect stalled ribosomes and targets the resulting nascent polypeptide chains for degradation.

siblings:protein quality control.name: Autophagy-mediated Protein Quality Control
siblings:protein quality control.description: The selective sequestration and degradation of large protein aggregates via the autophagic-lysosomal pathway, often referred to as aggrephagy.

siblings:protein quality control.name: Chloroplast Protein Quality Control
siblings:protein quality control.description: A system in plant cells that manages protein folding, assembly, and degradation within the chloroplast to maintain photosynthetic efficiency.

siblings:programmed cell death.name: Necroptosis
siblings:programmed cell death.description: A regulated form of necrosis mediated by the RIPK1-RIPK3-MLKL signaling pathway.

siblings:programmed cell death.name: Pyroptosis
siblings:programmed cell death.description: A highly inflammatory form of programmed cell death typically triggered by inflammasome activation and gasdermin pores.

siblings:programmed cell death.name: Ferroptosis
siblings:programmed cell death.description: An iron-dependent form of cell death driven by the lethal accumulation of lipid hydroperoxides.

siblings:programmed cell death.name: Autophagy-dependent cell death
siblings:programmed cell death.description: A type of regulated cell death that mechanistically relies upon the autophagic machinery.

siblings:programmed cell death.name: Parthanatos
siblings:programmed cell death.description: A form of programmed cell death characterized by hyperactivation of PARP1 and translocation of AIF from mitochondria to the nucleus.

siblings:programmed cell death.name: Entosis
siblings:programmed cell death.description: A process of cell-in-cell death where one cell is engulfed and subsequently killed by a neighboring cell.

siblings:cell death signaling.name: Apoptosis Signaling
siblings:cell death signaling.description: A regulated form of programmed cell death characterized by DNA fragmentation and caspase activation.

siblings:cell death signaling.name: Necroptosis Signaling
siblings:cell death signaling.description: A caspase-independent form of regulated cell death involving RIPK1, RIPK3, and MLKL proteins.

siblings:cell death signaling.name: Pyroptosis Signaling
siblings:cell death signaling.description: A highly inflammatory form of programmed cell death triggered by inflammasome activation and gasdermin pore formation.

siblings:cell death signaling.name: Ferroptosis Signaling
siblings:cell death signaling.description: An iron-dependent form of regulated cell death driven by the accumulation of lipid peroxides.

siblings:cell death signaling.name: Parthanatos Signaling
siblings:cell death signaling.description: A cell death pathway initiated by PARP1 overactivation leading to the translocation of apoptosis-inducing factor from mitochondria.

siblings:cell death signaling.name: Autophagy-dependent Cell Death
siblings:cell death signaling.description: A type of regulated cell death that mechanistically depends on the autophagic machinery of the cell.

siblings:cell death signaling.name: Entotic Cell Death
siblings:cell death signaling.description: A process where one cell invades or is engulfed by another, followed by its destruction within the lysosome of the host cell.

siblings:cellular stress response.name: Heat Shock Response
siblings:cellular stress response.description: A conserved mechanism involving the synthesis of chaperones to protect proteins from denaturing due to high temperatures or proteotoxicity.

siblings:cellular stress response.name: Unfolded Protein Response
siblings:cellular stress response.description: A cellular stress response related to the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) triggered by an accumulation of unfolded or misfolded proteins in the ER lumen.

siblings:cellular stress response.name: DNA Damage Response
siblings:cellular stress response.description: A signal transduction pathway that detects DNA lesions and coordinates repair, cell cycle checkpoints, or apoptosis to maintain genomic integrity.

siblings:cellular stress response.name: Hypoxia Response
siblings:cellular stress response.description: A regulatory mechanism, primarily mediated by Hypoxia-Inducible Factors (HIFs), that allows cells to adapt to low oxygen environments.

siblings:cellular stress response.name: Osmotic Stress Response
siblings:cellular stress response.description: The cellular processes used to maintain water and ion balance in response to changes in the surrounding solute concentration.

siblings:cellular stress response.name: Cold Stress Response
siblings:cellular stress response.description: Physiological and molecular adjustments, such as changes in membrane fluidity and RNA chaperone activity, to survive low temperatures.

siblings:macroautophagy.name: Non-selective Macroautophagy
siblings:macroautophagy.description: The bulk, stochastic degradation of cytoplasmic components often triggered by nutrient starvation.

siblings:macroautophagy.name: Macroautophagy Initiation
siblings:macroautophagy.description: The upstream signaling cascade involving the ULK1/2 complex that triggers phagophore formation.

siblings:macroautophagy.name: Vesicle Nucleation
siblings:macroautophagy.description: The recruitment of the Class III PI3K complex to the site of autophagosome assembly.

siblings:macroautophagy.name: Autophagosome Formation
siblings:macroautophagy.description: The expansion and elongation of the phagophore membrane to sequester cytoplasmic cargo.

siblings:macroautophagy.name: Autophagosome Maturation
siblings:macroautophagy.description: The processing of the completed autophagosome, including the removal of certain ATG proteins.

siblings:macroautophagy.name: Autophagosome-Lysosome Fusion
siblings:macroautophagy.description: The merging of the autophagosomal outer membrane with the lysosomal membrane to form an autolysosome.

siblings:macroautophagy.name: Autophagic Cargo Degradation
siblings:macroautophagy.description: The enzymatic breakdown of sequestered materials by lysosomal hydrolases within the autolysosome.

siblings:microtubule-based transport.name: Intraflagellar Transport
siblings:microtubule-based transport.description: The bidirectional movement of protein complexes along the doublet microtubules of cilia and flagella.

siblings:microtubule-based transport.name: Dendritic Transport
siblings:microtubule-based transport.description: The microtubule-based movement of organelles and protein complexes specifically within the dendritic compartments of neurons.

siblings:microtubule-based transport.name: Chromosome Movement
siblings:microtubule-based transport.description: The transport of sister chromatids and homologous chromosomes along the mitotic or meiotic spindle during cell division.

siblings:microtubule-based transport.name: Melanosome Transport
siblings:microtubule-based transport.description: The bidirectional distribution of pigment-containing organelles along microtubules in melanophores and melanocytes.

siblings:microtubule-based transport.name: Organelle Transport
siblings:microtubule-based transport.description: The trafficking of mitochondria, lysosomes, and secretory vesicles in the cytoplasm of non-neuronal cells.

siblings:microtubule-based transport.name: mRNA Transport
siblings:microtubule-based transport.description: The directed movement of ribonucleoprotein complexes along microtubules to facilitate localized protein synthesis.

siblings:cytoskeleton organization.name: Actin Cytoskeleton Organization
siblings:cytoskeleton organization.description: Processes that result in the assembly, arrangement, or disassembly of the actin cytoskeleton.

siblings:cytoskeleton organization.name: Intermediate Filament Organization
siblings:cytoskeleton organization.description: Processes that result in the assembly, arrangement, or disassembly of intermediate filaments.

siblings:cytoskeleton organization.name: Centrosome Organization
siblings:cytoskeleton organization.description: Processes that lead to the assembly and maintenance of the centrosome, which serves as a major microtubule organizing center.

siblings:cytoskeleton organization.name: Actin Filament Organization
siblings:cytoskeleton organization.description: A subprocess specifically focused on the formation and maintenance of individual actin filaments.

siblings:cytoskeleton organization.name: Septin Cytoskeleton Organization
siblings:cytoskeleton organization.description: Processes that result in the assembly and maintenance of the septin cytoskeleton.

siblings:cytoskeleton organization.name: Myofibril Assembly
siblings:cytoskeleton organization.description: The process by which contractile myofibrils are organized and formed within muscle cells.

siblings:genome maintenance.name: DNA Repair Mechanisms
siblings:genome maintenance.description: Processes such as Base Excision Repair, Nucleotide Excision Repair, and Mismatch Repair that fix specific DNA lesions.

siblings:genome maintenance.name: DNA Damage Signaling
siblings:genome maintenance.description: The detection of genomic insults and the subsequent activation of signaling cascades like the ATM/ATR pathways.

siblings:genome maintenance.name: Telomere Maintenance
siblings:genome maintenance.description: Processes that preserve the structural integrity and length of chromosomal ends to prevent genomic instability.

siblings:genome maintenance.name: DNA Replication Fidelity
siblings:genome maintenance.description: Mechanisms that ensure accurate DNA copying and the stabilization of stalled or collapsed replication forks.

siblings:genome maintenance.name: Chromatin Remodeling
siblings:genome maintenance.description: Dynamic modification of chromatin structure to facilitate access for repair enzymes and preserve epigenetic information.

siblings:genome maintenance.name: Apoptosis Induction
siblings:genome maintenance.description: Programmed cell death pathways triggered when genomic damage is irreparable, preventing the propagation of mutations.

siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.name: Actin Dynamics
siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.description: The regulated assembly, disassembly, and branching of actin filaments that drive cell shape and movement.

siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.name: Microtubule Dynamics
siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.description: The polymerization, depolymerization, and stabilization of microtubules essential for intracellular transport and mitosis.

siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.name: Cytoskeletal Motor Activity
siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.description: The function of motor proteins such as kinesins, dyneins, and myosins in transporting cargo and generating force.

siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.name: Centrosome and Spindle Dynamics
siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.description: The organization and regulation of microtubule-organizing centers and the mitotic spindle apparatus.

siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.name: Intermediate Filament Dynamics
siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.description: The assembly and remodeling of intermediate filaments providing mechanical stability and structural integrity.

siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.name: Cilium and Flagellum Assembly
siblings:cytoskeletal dynamics.description: The specialized organization of microtubules into axonemes for sensory or locomotive organelles.

siblings:regulation of gene expression.name: Post-transcriptional Regulation
siblings:regulation of gene expression.description: Control of gene expression at the RNA level, including splicing, nuclear export, and mRNA stability.

siblings:regulation of gene expression.name: Translational Regulation
siblings:regulation of gene expression.description: Mechanisms that modulate the rate and efficiency of protein synthesis from mRNA templates.

siblings:regulation of gene expression.name: Post-translational Regulation
siblings:regulation of gene expression.description: Control of protein activity, stability, and localization through chemical modifications or degradation.

siblings:regulation of gene expression.name: Epigenetic Regulation
siblings:regulation of gene expression.description: Heritable changes in gene expression that do not involve alterations to the underlying DNA sequence.

siblings:regulation of gene expression.name: Chromatin Remodeling
siblings:regulation of gene expression.description: The dynamic modification of chromatin architecture to allow or restrict access of transcriptional machinery to DNA.

siblings:regulation of gene expression.name: RNA Transport and Localization
siblings:regulation of gene expression.description: Regulated movement of mRNA molecules from the nucleus to specific sites in the cytoplasm.

siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.name: Toll-like Receptor (TLR) Signaling
siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.description: Transmembrane receptors that recognize a wide range of PAMPs and activate NF-kB and IRF pathways.

siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.name: RIG-I-like Receptor (RLR) Signaling
siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.description: Cytosolic sensors of viral RNA that induce the production of Type I interferons and proinflammatory cytokines.

siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.name: C-type Lectin Receptor (CLR) Signaling
siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.description: Receptors that detect carbohydrate motifs on pathogens, particularly fungi, to initiate immune responses.

siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.name: cGAS-STING Signaling
siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.description: A pathway that senses cytosolic double-stranded DNA and triggers a robust antiviral and inflammatory response.

siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.name: NOD-like Receptor (NLR) Signaling (non-inflammasome)
siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.description: Intracellular sensors like NOD1 and NOD2 that activate NF-kB and MAPK pathways independently of the inflammasome.

siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.name: Scavenger Receptor Signaling
siblings:pattern recognition receptor (p.description: Cell surface receptors that recognize modified lipoproteins and various microbial ligands to facilitate phagocytosis.

siblings:selective macroautophagy.name: Pexophagy
siblings:selective macroautophagy.description: The selective degradation of peroxisomes via the autophagic machinery.

siblings:selective macroautophagy.name: ER-phagy
siblings:selective macroautophagy.description: The selective sequestration and degradation of portions of the endoplasmic reticulum.

siblings:selective macroautophagy.name: Aggrephagy
siblings:selective macroautophagy.description: The selective removal of misfolded and ubiquitinated protein aggregates.

siblings:selective macroautophagy.name: Xenophagy
siblings:selective macroautophagy.description: The autophagic degradation of intracellular bacterial, viral, or protozoan pathogens.

siblings:selective macroautophagy.name: Ribophagy
siblings:selective macroautophagy.description: The selective degradation of ribosomes, often in response to nutrient starvation.

siblings:selective macroautophagy.name: Lipophagy
siblings:selective macroautophagy.description: The selective degradation of intracellular lipid droplets to regulate lipid metabolism.

siblings:selective macroautophagy.name: Lysophagy
siblings:selective macroautophagy.description: The selective autophagic clearance of damaged or ruptured lysosomes.

siblings:nuclear organization.name: Chromatin Organization
siblings:nuclear organization.description: Processes that result in the specification, formation, maintenance, or disassembly of chromatin structure within the nucleus.

siblings:nuclear organization.name: Nuclear Envelope Organization
siblings:nuclear organization.description: The assembly, disassembly, and maintenance of the double-membrane system that bounds the nucleus.

siblings:nuclear organization.name: Nuclear Pore Complex Organization
siblings:nuclear organization.description: The regulated assembly and spatial arrangement of the large protein complexes that bridge the nuclear envelope.

siblings:nuclear organization.name: Nuclear Lamina Organization
siblings:nuclear organization.description: The formation and structural maintenance of the dense fibrillar network of intermediate filaments on the inner nuclear membrane.

siblings:nuclear organization.name: Nucleolus Organization
siblings:nuclear organization.description: The specific processes involved in the formation and maintenance of the nucleolus, the site of ribosome biogenesis.

siblings:nuclear organization.name: Maintenance of Nuclear Architecture
siblings:nuclear organization.description: Global processes that ensure the mechanical integrity and spatial positioning of nuclear components.

siblings:signal transduction.name: Signaling by GPCR
siblings:signal transduction.description: Transduction involving G protein-coupled receptors that respond to external signals like hormones and neurotransmitters.

siblings:signal transduction.name: Signaling by Receptor Tyrosine Kinases
siblings:signal transduction.description: A process where high-affinity cell surface receptors for growth factors mediate cellular responses through phosphorylation.

siblings:signal transduction.name: Signaling by Nuclear Receptors
siblings:signal transduction.description: A class of proteins that function as ligand-activated transcription factors to regulate gene expression.

siblings:signal transduction.name: Signaling by TGF-beta
siblings:signal transduction.description: A pathway regulating cell growth, differentiation, and development across various tissue types.

siblings:signal transduction.name: Signaling by Wnt
siblings:signal transduction.description: A group of signal transduction pathways that control cell-to-cell communication during embryonic development and tissue maintenance.

siblings:signal transduction.name: Signaling by Notch
siblings:signal transduction.description: A highly conserved cell signaling system that regulates cell-fate determination through direct cell-to-cell contact.

siblings:signal transduction.name: Signaling by Interleukins
siblings:signal transduction.description: A subset of cytokine signaling that regulates the immune system response and inflammatory processes.

siblings:developmental signaling.name: Wnt Signaling Pathway
siblings:developmental signaling.description: Regulates cell proliferation, polarity, and fate determination during embryonic development.

siblings:developmental signaling.name: Notch Signaling Pathway
siblings:developmental signaling.description: Facilitates juxtacrine cell-to-cell communication to coordinate tissue patterning and differentiation.

siblings:developmental signaling.name: Hedgehog Signaling Pathway
siblings:developmental signaling.description: Crucial for embryonic pattern formation, organogenesis, and limb development.

siblings:developmental signaling.name: TGF-beta Signaling Pathway
siblings:developmental signaling.description: Controls growth, differentiation, and development in various tissues and organs.

siblings:developmental signaling.name: FGF Signaling Pathway
siblings:developmental signaling.description: Involved in mesoderm induction, neural patterning, and limb bud outgrowth.

siblings:developmental signaling.name: Hippo Signaling Pathway
siblings:developmental signaling.description: Regulates organ size by controlling cell proliferation and apoptosis.

siblings:developmental signaling.name: Eph-Ephrin Signaling
siblings:developmental signaling.description: Mediates cell-cell contact-dependent guidance in axon pathfinding and vascular development.

siblings:dna repair.name: Base Excision Repair (BER)
siblings:dna repair.description: A cellular mechanism that repairs damaged DNA bases resulting from oxidation, alkylation, or deamination.

siblings:dna repair.name: Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER)
siblings:dna repair.description: A versatile pathway that removes bulky, helix-distorting DNA lesions, such as those caused by UV radiation.

siblings:dna repair.name: Mismatch Repair (MMR)
siblings:dna repair.description: A system for recognizing and repairing erroneous insertion, deletion, and mis-incorporation of bases during DNA replication.

siblings:dna repair.name: Direct Reversal
siblings:dna repair.description: A simple repair mechanism that directly corrects specific chemical modifications to DNA bases without removing the DNA backbone.

siblings:dna repair.name: Interstrand Crosslink (ICL) Repair
siblings:dna repair.description: A complex repair process that resolves covalent linkages between the two strands of the DNA double helix.

siblings:dna repair.name: Translesion Synthesis (TLS)
siblings:dna repair.description: A damage tolerance pathway that utilizes specialized DNA polymerases to replicate across DNA lesions that stall standard polymerases.

siblings:innate immune signaling.name: Complement Cascade
siblings:innate immune signaling.description: A biochemical cascade of the innate immune system that enhances the ability of antibodies and phagocytic cells to clear microbes.

siblings:innate immune signaling.name: Type I Interferon Signaling
siblings:innate immune signaling.description: Signaling pathways triggered by IFN-alpha and IFN-beta that induce an antiviral state in host cells.

siblings:innate immune signaling.name: Natural Killer Cell Signaling
siblings:innate immune signaling.description: Pathways that regulate the activation, recruitment, and cytotoxic response of NK cells against pathogens.

siblings:innate immune signaling.name: Chemokine Signaling
siblings:innate immune signaling.description: Intracellular signaling that directs the migration and recruitment of innate immune cells to sites of infection or injury.

siblings:innate immune signaling.name: Phagocytosis and Phagosome Maturation
siblings:innate immune signaling.description: The process by which specialized cells engulf and digest large particles, such as pathogens or apoptotic cells.

siblings:innate immune signaling.name: Pro-inflammatory Cytokine Signaling
siblings:innate immune signaling.description: Signaling pathways activated by cytokines like TNF, IL-1, and IL-6 to coordinate the inflammatory response.

siblings:physiological signal transducti.name: Nervous System Signaling
siblings:physiological signal transducti.description: The transmission of chemical and electrical signals between neurons and across synapses to coordinate bodily functions.

siblings:physiological signal transducti.name: Endocrine Signaling
siblings:physiological signal transducti.description: The secretion of hormones into the circulatory system to regulate physiological processes in distant target organs.

siblings:physiological signal transducti.name: Sensory Signal Transduction
siblings:physiological signal transducti.description: The process by which specialized cells convert external physical or chemical stimuli into electrical impulses.

siblings:physiological signal transducti.name: Metabolic Signaling
siblings:physiological signal transducti.description: Intracellular pathways that sense and respond to nutrient availability and energy status to maintain homeostasis.

siblings:physiological signal transducti.name: Developmental Signaling
siblings:physiological signal transducti.description: Coordinated signaling pathways such as Wnt, Notch, and BMP that guide cell differentiation and tissue morphogenesis.

siblings:physiological signal transducti.name: Cardiovascular Signal Transduction
siblings:physiological signal transducti.description: Molecular signals regulating heart rate, myocardial contractility, and the maintenance of vascular tone.

siblings:physiological signal transducti.name: Musculoskeletal Signaling
siblings:physiological signal transducti.description: Signaling cascades that control skeletal muscle contraction, bone density maintenance, and joint repair mechanisms.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: ER-phagy
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: Selective autophagy of the endoplasmic reticulum used to degrade large protein aggregates or excess membrane components.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: Unfolded Protein Response (UPR)
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: A complex signaling pathway that detects protein misfolding and triggers adaptive measures to restore ER homeostasis.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: ER Retention and Retrieval
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: A mechanism that employs retention motifs and chaperones to keep unfolded or misfolded proteins from exiting the ER.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: Protein Folding and Refolding
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: The active process mediated by molecular chaperones like BiP to facilitate the correct native conformation of polypeptides.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: ER-to-Lysosome Degradation (ERLAD)
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: A degradation pathway that transports large misfolded protein complexes or aggregates directly to the lysosome, bypassing the proteasome.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: Glycoprotein Quality Control (CNX/CRT Cycle)
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: A specialized cycle involving calnexin and calreticulin that monitors and assists the folding of N-linked glycoproteins.

siblings:proteostasis.name: Ubiquitin-Proteasome System
siblings:proteostasis.description: The primary pathway for the selective degradation of misfolded or short-lived proteins via the 26S proteasome.

siblings:proteostasis.name: Autophagy-Lysosomal Pathway
siblings:proteostasis.description: A bulk degradation process that delivers cytoplasmic components and organelles to the lysosome for recycling.

siblings:proteostasis.name: Chaperone-Mediated Protein Folding
siblings:proteostasis.description: The use of molecular chaperones to assist nascent polypeptides in reaching their native functional conformation.

siblings:proteostasis.name: Endoplasmic Reticulum-Associated Degradation (ERAD)
siblings:proteostasis.description: A quality control mechanism that targets misfolded proteins in the ER for retrotranslocation and degradation.

siblings:proteostasis.name: Unfolded Protein Response (UPR)
siblings:proteostasis.description: A cellular stress response related to the endoplasmic reticulum that regulates protein synthesis and chaperone levels.

siblings:proteostasis.name: Protein Trafficking and Secretion
siblings:proteostasis.description: The regulated transport of proteins to their correct cellular compartments or the extracellular space.

siblings:proteostasis.name: Aggresome Formation and Clearance
siblings:proteostasis.description: The sequestration of insoluble protein aggregates into specialized structures for eventual autophagic clearance.

siblings:membrane & transport.name: Membrane Organization
siblings:membrane & transport.description: The process of assembling, arranging, or disassembling the constituent parts of a biological membrane.

siblings:membrane & transport.name: Signal Transduction
siblings:membrane & transport.description: The transmission of molecular signals from a cell's exterior to its interior, ensuring appropriate responses.

siblings:membrane & transport.name: Vesicle-Mediated Transport
siblings:membrane & transport.description: The movement of molecules between different cellular compartments via membrane-bound carriers.

siblings:membrane & transport.name: Ion Transport
siblings:membrane & transport.description: The movement of ions across biological membranes through specialized channels, carriers, or pumps.

siblings:membrane & transport.name: Protein Targeting and Sorting
siblings:membrane & transport.description: The mechanisms by which proteins are directed to their correct intracellular or extracellular locations.

siblings:membrane & transport.name: Membrane Potential Maintenance
siblings:membrane & transport.description: The regulatory processes that maintain the electrochemical gradient across the plasma membrane.

siblings:morphogen signaling.name: Wnt Signaling Pathway
siblings:morphogen signaling.description: Regulates cell fate, organogenesis, and embryonic development through beta-catenin stabilization or non-canonical cascades.

siblings:morphogen signaling.name: TGF-beta Signaling Pathway
siblings:morphogen signaling.description: Includes BMPs and Activins, influencing cell growth, differentiation, and dorsal-ventral axis specification during development.

siblings:morphogen signaling.name: Notch Signaling Pathway
siblings:morphogen signaling.description: A highly conserved cell-to-cell signaling mechanism that determines cell fate decisions and maintains stem cell populations.

siblings:morphogen signaling.name: Fibroblast Growth Factor (FGF) Signaling Pathway
siblings:morphogen signaling.description: Plays essential roles in gastrulation, limb development, and neural patterning via receptor tyrosine kinases.

siblings:morphogen signaling.name: Retinoic Acid (RA) Signaling Pathway
siblings:morphogen signaling.description: A small molecule morphogen that regulates anterior-posterior patterning and organ development by binding to nuclear receptors.

siblings:morphogen signaling.name: Hippo Signaling Pathway
siblings:morphogen signaling.description: Coordinates cell proliferation and apoptosis to control organ size and maintain tissue homeostasis during morphogenesis.

siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.name: Actin-mediated transport
siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.description: Movement of intracellular cargo, such as vesicles and organelles, along actin filaments primarily using myosin motor proteins.

siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.name: Myosin-dependent transport
siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.description: A specialized form of transport along microfilaments that utilizes the ATP-driven activity of the myosin motor superfamily.

siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.name: Intermediate filament-based transport
siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.description: The transport and mechanical positioning of cellular components, including the nucleus and mitochondria, facilitated by intermediate filaments.

siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.name: Actin-dependent organelle positioning
siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.description: The specific movement and anchoring of organelles to defined cellular locations using the actin cytoskeleton network.

siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.name: Microfilament-based cargo transport
siblings:cytoskeleton-dependent transpor.description: The translocation of molecular complexes and vesicles through the cytoplasm along microfilaments.

siblings:protein degradation.name: Macroautophagy
siblings:protein degradation.description: A pathway where cytoplasmic components are sequestered within double-membrane vesicles called autophagosomes and delivered to lysosomes for degradation.

siblings:protein degradation.name: Chaperone-Mediated Autophagy
siblings:protein degradation.description: A selective degradation process where specific cytosolic proteins containing a KFERQ-like motif are directly translocated across the lysosomal membrane.

siblings:protein degradation.name: Calpain System
siblings:protein degradation.description: A non-lysosomal, calcium-dependent proteolytic system involving cysteine proteases that facilitate limited cleavage of proteins for signaling and structural purposes.

siblings:protein degradation.name: Endosomal-Lysosomal Pathway
siblings:protein degradation.description: The degradation route for extracellular and plasma membrane proteins internalized through endocytosis and subsequently digested by lysosomal hydrolases.

siblings:protein degradation.name: Caspase Pathway
siblings:protein degradation.description: A family of protease enzymes that play essential roles in programmed cell death and inflammation through highly specific protein cleavage.

siblings:protein degradation.name: Microautophagy
siblings:protein degradation.description: A process involving the direct engulfment of cytoplasmic material by the lysosomal or vacuolar membrane through invagination or protrusion.

siblings:protein degradation.name: Matrix Metalloproteinase Pathway
siblings:protein degradation.description: A system of zinc-dependent endopeptidases responsible for the degradation and remodeling of extracellular matrix proteins.

siblings:cellular transport.name: Active Transport
siblings:cellular transport.description: The movement of molecules across a cell membrane from a region of lower concentration to a region of higher concentration using energy.

siblings:cellular transport.name: Passive Transport
siblings:cellular transport.description: The movement of substances across cell membranes without the requirement of energy input.

siblings:cellular transport.name: Endocytosis
siblings:cellular transport.description: A cellular process in which substances are brought into the cell by engulfing them in a vesicle.

siblings:cellular transport.name: Exocytosis
siblings:cellular transport.description: A process by which the contents of a cell vacuole are released to the exterior through fusion of the vacuole membrane with the cell membrane.

siblings:cellular transport.name: Transcytosis
siblings:cellular transport.description: A type of transcellular transport in which various macromolecules are transported across the interior of a cell.

siblings:cellular transport.name: Nucleocytoplasmic Transport
siblings:cellular transport.description: The movement of macromolecules between the cell nucleus and the cytoplasm across the nuclear envelope.

siblings:cellular signaling.name: Intracellular Signaling
siblings:cellular signaling.description: The process of communication and signal relay that occurs strictly within the internal environment of a single cell.

siblings:cellular signaling.name: Signal Transduction
siblings:cellular signaling.description: The biochemical cascades through which a cell converts an extracellular signal into a specific functional response.

siblings:cellular signaling.name: Sensory Transduction
siblings:cellular signaling.description: The transformation of physical or chemical environmental stimuli, such as light or sound, into cellular electrochemical signals.

siblings:cellular signaling.name: Environmental Sensing
siblings:cellular signaling.description: The mechanisms by which cells detect and respond to non-biological external cues including pH, temperature, and nutrient concentration.

siblings:cellular signaling.name: Nuclear Receptor Signaling
siblings:cellular signaling.description: A specialized signaling pathway involving lipophilic ligands that activate intracellular receptors to modulate gene expression directly.

siblings:cellular signaling.name: Metabolic Signaling
siblings:cellular signaling.description: Regulatory pathways where cellular metabolic intermediates or flux levels act as signaling molecules to control homeostasis.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.name: Unfolded Protein Response (UPR)
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.description: An adaptive signaling pathway that senses accumulation of misfolded proteins and coordinates cellular responses to restore ER homeostasis.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.name: Calnexin/Calreticulin (CNX/CRT) Cycle
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.description: A glycan-dependent quality control system that monitors the folding state of glycoproteins and prevents the exit of immature proteins.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.name: ER-phagy
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.description: The selective degradation of the endoplasmic reticulum via autophagy to clear large protein aggregates and damaged membrane sections.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.name: ER-to-Lysosome Degradation (ERLAD)
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.description: A pathway that directs misfolded proteins or aggregates to the endolysosomal system for degradation, bypassing the proteasome.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.name: ER Selective Export
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.description: A checkpoint mechanism at ER exit sites that ensures only correctly folded and assembled proteins are packaged into COPII transport vesicles.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.name: ER Retrieval
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum protein q.description: A sorting process that recognizes escaped ER-resident proteins in the Golgi and returns them to the ER lumen using COPI-coated vesicles.

siblings:excision repair.name: Base Excision Repair
siblings:excision repair.description: A mechanism that removes small, non-helix-distorting damaged bases through the action of DNA glycosylases and AP endonucleases.

siblings:excision repair.name: Mismatch Repair
siblings:excision repair.description: A system that recognizes and corrects erroneous insertions, deletions, and mis-incorporations of bases that occur during DNA replication.

siblings:excision repair.name: Ribonucleotide Excision Repair
siblings:excision repair.description: A specialized pathway responsible for removing ribonucleotides mistakenly incorporated into the DNA strand by DNA polymerases.

siblings:excision repair.name: Alternative Excision Repair
siblings:excision repair.description: An endonucleolytic pathway that serves as a backup or alternative to standard NER and BER, typically involving enzymes like UV-damage endonuclease (UVDE).

siblings:excision repair.name: Single-Strand Break Repair
siblings:excision repair.description: A pathway closely related to BER that specifically handles the repair of gaps and breaks in the sugar-phosphate backbone of a single DNA strand.

siblings:immune system signaling.name: Adaptive Immune Signaling
siblings:immune system signaling.description: Pathways mediating the highly specific, memory-based immune response involving T and B lymphocytes.

siblings:immune system signaling.name: Cytokine Signaling
siblings:immune system signaling.description: Intercellular communication pathways mediated by cytokines that regulate the intensity and duration of the immune response.

siblings:immune system signaling.name: Chemokine Signaling
siblings:immune system signaling.description: Signaling cascades that coordinate the directional migration and localization of leukocytes within tissues.

siblings:immune system signaling.name: Antigen Processing and Presentation
siblings:immune system signaling.description: The specialized signaling process for the capture, digestion, and display of antigens on MHC molecules for immune recognition.

siblings:immune system signaling.name: T Cell Receptor (TCR) Signaling
siblings:immune system signaling.description: Intracellular signaling events triggered by the recognition of peptide-MHC complexes by T cells.

siblings:immune system signaling.name: B Cell Receptor (BCR) Signaling
siblings:immune system signaling.description: Signaling pathways initiated by antigen binding to surface immunoglobulins on B cells, leading to activation and differentiation.

siblings:immune system signaling.name: Leukocyte Transendothelial Migration Signaling
siblings:immune system signaling.description: Signaling events that facilitate the movement of immune cells from the bloodstream across the vascular wall into sites of infection.

siblings:cell death.name: Accidental Cell Death
siblings:cell death.description: An instantaneous and uncontrolled form of cell death resulting from severe physical, chemical, or mechanical insults.

siblings:cell death.name: Mitotic Catastrophe
siblings:cell death.description: A regulated mechanism of cell death triggered by aberrant mitosis, often resulting from DNA damage or spindle dysfunction.

siblings:cell death.name: Autophagic Cell Death
siblings:cell death.description: A form of cell death that is mechanistically dependent on the cell's autophagic machinery for cytoplasm degradation.

siblings:cell death.name: Cornification
siblings:cell death.description: A specialized terminal differentiation process in epithelial cells resulting in the formation of a dead, protective barrier layer.

siblings:cell death.name: Entosis
siblings:cell death.description: A non-apoptotic cell death pathway where one viable cell is engulfed by another and subsequently degraded within a lysosome.

siblings:cell death.name: Wallerian Degeneration
siblings:cell death.description: An active, regulated process of axon degeneration that occurs distal to the site of a nerve lesion.

siblings:cell death.name: Necrotic Cell Death
siblings:cell death.description: A type of cell death characterized by cellular swelling and the early loss of plasma membrane integrity, distinct from programmed apoptosis.

siblings:cellular responses to external.name: Cellular Senescence
siblings:cellular responses to external.description: A state of stable cell cycle arrest that prevents the proliferation of damaged or aged cells in response to various stressors.

siblings:cellular responses to external.name: Cellular response to starvation
siblings:cellular responses to external.description: Adaptive metabolic and signaling changes, such as the induction of autophagy, triggered by the deprivation of essential nutrients.

siblings:cellular responses to external.name: Response to metal ions
siblings:cellular responses to external.description: The molecular processes that regulate the uptake, sequestration, and detoxification of metal ions like zinc, copper, and iron.

siblings:cellular responses to external.name: Cellular response to radiation
siblings:cellular responses to external.description: The signaling and DNA repair pathways activated in response to exposure to external ionizing or ultraviolet radiation.

siblings:cellular responses to external.name: Cellular response to mechanical stimulus
siblings:cellular responses to external.description: The process of mechanotransduction where physical environmental forces are converted into intracellular biochemical signals.

siblings:cellular responses to external.name: Cellular response to oxygen levels
siblings:cellular responses to external.description: Adaptive homeostatic adjustments to gene expression and metabolism in response to fluctuations in ambient oxygen concentrations.

siblings:cellular responses to external.name: Cellular response to chemical stimulus
siblings:cellular responses to external.description: The molecular and physiological changes initiated in a cell upon the detection of extracellular xenobiotics or signaling molecules.

siblings:nucleotide excision repair.name: Global Genomic Nucleotide Excision Repair (GG-NER)
siblings:nucleotide excision repair.description: A repair pathway that surveys the entire genome for helix-distorting DNA lesions regardless of transcriptional activity.

siblings:nucleotide excision repair.name: Dual Incision in NER
siblings:nucleotide excision repair.description: The coordinated stage where specific endonucleases cleave the damaged DNA strand on both the 5' and 3' sides of the lesion.

siblings:nucleotide excision repair.name: Gap-filling DNA Repair Synthesis
siblings:nucleotide excision repair.description: The phase where DNA polymerases synthesize new DNA to fill the gap using the undamaged complementary strand as a template.

siblings:nucleotide excision repair.name: DNA Ligation in NER
siblings:nucleotide excision repair.description: The final step where DNA ligase seals the phosphodiester backbone to complete the repair process.

siblings:nucleotide excision repair.name: DNA Damage Recognition in GG-NER
siblings:nucleotide excision repair.description: The specific sub-process involving the XPC and DDB complexes that identify lesions throughout the genome.

siblings:nucleotide excision repair.name: UvrABC-mediated Nucleotide Excision Repair
siblings:nucleotide excision repair.description: The evolutionary conserved bacterial NER pathway utilizing the UvrA, UvrB, and UvrC protein complex.

siblings:tissue-specific signaling.name: Neural Signaling
siblings:tissue-specific signaling.description: Signaling pathways specific to neurons and glial cells that regulate synaptic plasticity and neurogenesis.

siblings:tissue-specific signaling.name: Cardiovascular Signaling
siblings:tissue-specific signaling.description: Molecular interactions governing heart muscle contraction, vascular tone, and endothelial health.

siblings:tissue-specific signaling.name: Immune Signaling
siblings:tissue-specific signaling.description: Tissue-specific cascades regulating the maturation, activation, and coordination of leukocytes.

siblings:tissue-specific signaling.name: Epithelial Signaling
siblings:tissue-specific signaling.description: Mechanisms controlling cell polarity, barrier function, and regeneration in epithelial tissues.

siblings:tissue-specific signaling.name: Endocrine Signaling
siblings:tissue-specific signaling.description: Specialized signaling involving hormone production and tissue-specific responses within glandular systems.

siblings:tissue-specific signaling.name: Adipose Tissue Signaling
siblings:tissue-specific signaling.description: Regulatory pathways controlling lipid metabolism, thermogenesis, and the secretion of adipokines.

siblings:rna metabolism.name: Transcription
siblings:rna metabolism.description: The biochemical process of synthesizing an RNA molecule from a DNA template.

siblings:rna metabolism.name: RNA Processing
siblings:rna metabolism.description: The series of covalent modifications including 5' capping, splicing, and 3' polyadenylation that convert pre-mRNA into mature mRNA.

siblings:rna metabolism.name: RNA Export
siblings:rna metabolism.description: The regulated transport of RNA molecules from the nucleus to the cytoplasm through nuclear pore complexes.

siblings:rna metabolism.name: rRNA Metabolism
siblings:rna metabolism.description: The synthesis, nucleolar processing, and assembly of ribosomal RNAs into functional subunits.

siblings:rna metabolism.name: tRNA Metabolism
siblings:rna metabolism.description: The multi-step process involving the synthesis, maturation, and modification of transfer RNAs.

siblings:rna metabolism.name: RNA Editing
siblings:rna metabolism.description: The post-transcriptional process by which the nucleotide sequence of an RNA molecule is altered via insertion, deletion, or substitution.

siblings:rna metabolism.name: Small RNA Biogenesis
siblings:rna metabolism.description: The pathway responsible for the production and processing of regulatory non-coding RNAs like miRNA and siRNA.

siblings:ion transport.name: Sodium Ion Transport
siblings:ion transport.description: The directed movement of sodium ions across biological membranes via specific channels or pumps.

siblings:ion transport.name: Potassium Ion Transport
siblings:ion transport.description: The movement of potassium ions through membrane proteins, essential for cellular excitability and volume regulation.

siblings:ion transport.name: Calcium Ion Transport
siblings:ion transport.description: The regulated movement of calcium ions into or out of cells or organelles, serving as a critical secondary messenger.

siblings:ion transport.name: Chloride Ion Transport
siblings:ion transport.description: The passage of chloride ions across membranes to regulate osmotic pressure, fluid secretion, and cell volume.

siblings:ion transport.name: Proton Transport
siblings:ion transport.description: The movement of hydrogen ions across membranes to create electrochemical gradients and maintain pH homeostasis.

siblings:ion transport.name: Anion Transport
siblings:ion transport.description: The directed movement of various negatively charged ions across a cell membrane or between compartments.

siblings:ion transport.name: Transition Metal Ion Transport
siblings:ion transport.description: The specific movement of trace metal ions such as zinc, iron, or copper required for enzymatic functions.

siblings:dna replication.name: DNA Replication Initiation
siblings:dna replication.description: The process of unwinding the DNA double helix and synthesizing an RNA primer to begin DNA synthesis.

siblings:dna replication.name: DNA Strand Elongation
siblings:dna replication.description: The rapid addition of dNTPs to the 3' end of the growing DNA strand by DNA polymerases.

siblings:dna replication.name: Leading Strand Synthesis
siblings:dna replication.description: Continuous synthesis of the new DNA strand in the 5' to 3' direction toward the replication fork.

siblings:dna replication.name: Lagging Strand Synthesis
siblings:dna replication.description: Discontinuous synthesis of DNA in short segments called Okazaki fragments away from the replication fork.

siblings:dna replication.name: Okazaki Fragment Processing
siblings:dna replication.description: The removal of RNA primers and the ligation of DNA segments on the lagging strand.

siblings:dna replication.name: DNA Replication Termination
siblings:dna replication.description: The completion of DNA synthesis and the disassembly of the replication machinery at specific termination sites.

siblings:dna replication.name: DNA Replication Pre-Initiation
siblings:dna replication.description: The assembly of the pre-replicative complex at the origin of replication prior to DNA synthesis.

siblings:microtubule dynamics.name: Microtubule Nucleation
siblings:microtubule dynamics.description: The initial formation of microtubules from tubulin dimers, primarily occurring at microtubule-organizing centers.

siblings:microtubule dynamics.name: Microtubule Assembly and Disassembly
siblings:microtubule dynamics.description: The dynamic processes of polymerization and depolymerization that allow microtubules to grow and shrink.

siblings:microtubule dynamics.name: Microtubule-Based Cargo Transport
siblings:microtubule dynamics.description: The intracellular movement of organelles, vesicles, and macromolecules along microtubules by motor proteins.

siblings:microtubule dynamics.name: Ciliary and Flagellar Dynamics
siblings:microtubule dynamics.description: The assembly and regulation of the microtubule-based axoneme in motile and primary cilia.

siblings:microtubule dynamics.name: Regulation of Microtubule Stability
siblings:microtubule dynamics.description: The control of microtubule turnover and persistence mediated by microtubule-associated proteins and modifications.

siblings:microtubule dynamics.name: Microtubule Reorganization in Neurons
siblings:microtubule dynamics.description: Specific dynamics involved in the formation and maintenance of axonal and dendritic microtubule arrays.

siblings:protein transport.name: Nucleocytoplasmic Transport
siblings:protein transport.description: The regulated movement of proteins and RNA between the cell nucleus and the cytoplasm via nuclear pore complexes.

siblings:protein transport.name: Transmembrane Transport
siblings:protein transport.description: The directed movement of proteins across a biological membrane, typically facilitated by translocons.

siblings:protein transport.name: Vesicular Transport
siblings:protein transport.description: The movement of proteins between different cellular compartments using membrane-bound transport vesicles.

siblings:protein transport.name: Protein Secretion
siblings:protein transport.description: The process of exporting proteins from the internal cellular environment to the extracellular space.

siblings:protein transport.name: Retrograde Transport
siblings:protein transport.description: The movement of proteins in the reverse direction of the secretory pathway, such as from the Golgi back to the endoplasmic reticulum.

siblings:protein transport.name: Anterograde Transport
siblings:protein transport.description: The forward movement of proteins along the secretory pathway, specifically from the endoplasmic reticulum to the Golgi apparatus.

siblings:protein processing.name: Protein Folding
siblings:protein processing.description: The physical process by which a polypeptide chain acquires its functional three-dimensional conformation.

siblings:protein processing.name: Post-translational Modification
siblings:protein processing.description: The covalent and enzymatic modification of proteins following protein biosynthesis to influence function or stability.

siblings:protein processing.name: Protein Translocation
siblings:protein processing.description: The movement of proteins across or into biological membranes to reach specific cellular compartments.

siblings:protein processing.name: Proteolytic Processing
siblings:protein processing.description: The specific cleavage of peptide bonds in a precursor protein to generate active enzymes or signaling molecules.

siblings:protein processing.name: Protein Degradation
siblings:protein processing.description: The regulated breakdown of proteins into amino acids or smaller peptides for quality control and cellular homeostasis.

siblings:protein processing.name: Protein Sorting
siblings:protein processing.description: The identification and delivery of proteins to their correct subcellular locations or for secretion.

siblings:chromosome maintenance.name: DNA Replication
siblings:chromosome maintenance.description: The process of duplicating the genetic material to ensure accurate inheritance during cell division.

siblings:chromosome maintenance.name: Chromatin Organization
siblings:chromosome maintenance.description: The structural arrangement and modification of DNA and histones into functional chromatin units.

siblings:chromosome maintenance.name: Chromosome Condensation
siblings:chromosome maintenance.description: The compacting of thin chromatin strands into discrete, manageable chromosomes during mitosis or meiosis.

siblings:chromosome maintenance.name: Sister Chromatid Cohesion
siblings:chromosome maintenance.description: The mechanism that keeps identical sister chromatids physically paired until their separation in anaphase.

siblings:chromosome maintenance.name: Kinetochore Assembly
siblings:chromosome maintenance.description: The formation of the multi-protein complex on centromeric DNA that facilitates spindle fiber attachment.

siblings:chromosome maintenance.name: DNA Repair
siblings:chromosome maintenance.description: The collection of biological pathways that identify and correct damage to DNA molecules.

siblings:cytokine signaling.name: Signaling by Interferons
siblings:cytokine signaling.description: Pathways initiated by interferon binding that induce antiviral, antiproliferative, and immunomodulatory responses.

siblings:cytokine signaling.name: TNF signaling
siblings:cytokine signaling.description: Signal transduction pathways mediated by Tumor Necrosis Factors involved in inflammation and apoptosis.

siblings:cytokine signaling.name: Chemokine receptors bind chemokines
siblings:cytokine signaling.description: Signaling through G protein-coupled receptors that directs leukocyte migration and activation.

siblings:cytokine signaling.name: Signaling by TGF-beta family members
siblings:cytokine signaling.description: Intracellular signaling regulating cell growth, differentiation, and development through SMAD proteins.

siblings:cytokine signaling.name: Signaling by Erythropoietin
siblings:cytokine signaling.description: Cytokine signaling pathway essential for the survival and proliferation of erythroid progenitor cells.

siblings:cytokine signaling.name: FLT3 signaling
siblings:cytokine signaling.description: Signaling involving the FLT3 receptor tyrosine kinase which is critical for hematopoietic stem cell development.

siblings:cytokine signaling.name: Signaling by CSF
siblings:cytokine signaling.description: Colony Stimulating Factor pathways that regulate the production and function of myeloid cells.

siblings:protein folding.name: Folding of Actin by CCT/TRiC
siblings:protein folding.description: The specialized process by which actin monomers are folded into their native state by the chaperonin CCT/TRiC complex.

siblings:protein folding.name: Folding of Tubulin by CCT/TRiC
siblings:protein folding.description: The chaperonin-assisted maturation of alpha and beta tubulin subunits into functional dimers.

siblings:protein folding.name: Prefoldin-mediated transfer of substrate to CCT/TRiC
siblings:protein folding.description: The mechanism by which the prefoldin complex captures nascent polypeptides and delivers them to the CCT/TRiC chaperonin.

siblings:protein folding.name: Protein folding in the endoplasmic reticulum
siblings:protein folding.description: The process of folding and assembly of newly synthesized proteins within the lumen of the endoplasmic reticulum.

siblings:protein folding.name: Co-translational protein folding
siblings:protein folding.description: Protein folding that occurs simultaneously with the translation of the polypeptide chain at the ribosome.

siblings:protein folding.name: Post-translational protein folding
siblings:protein folding.description: The process where polypeptide chains achieve their functional tertiary structure after complete release from the ribosome.

siblings:protein folding.name: Small-molecule-mediated protein folding
siblings:protein folding.description: The stabilization or folding of proteins facilitated by specific small-molecule ligands or chemical chaperones.

siblings:microtubule-based processes.name: Microtubule-based transport
siblings:microtubule-based processes.description: The movement of organelles, vesicles, or proteins along microtubules via molecular motors.

siblings:microtubule-based processes.name: Mitotic spindle organization
siblings:microtubule-based processes.description: The process of assembling and spatial arrangement of microtubules to form the mitotic spindle.

siblings:microtubule-based processes.name: Microtubule cytoskeleton organization
siblings:microtubule-based processes.description: The formation, maintenance, and spatial arrangement of the microtubule network within a cell.

siblings:microtubule-based processes.name: Chromosome segregation
siblings:microtubule-based processes.description: The microtubule-mediated physical movement of chromosomes to opposite cell poles.

siblings:microtubule-based processes.name: Microtubule nucleation
siblings:microtubule-based processes.description: The initiation of microtubule polymer formation from tubulin dimers at specific cellular sites.

siblings:microtubule-based processes.name: Organelle positioning
siblings:microtubule-based processes.description: The active distribution and maintenance of organelle locations using microtubule-based motor proteins.

siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.name: Glycolysis / Gluconeogenesis
siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.description: The metabolic pathways that break down glucose for energy and synthesize glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors.

siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.name: Pentose phosphate pathway
siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.description: A pathway parallel to glycolysis that generates NADPH and pentoses, as well as ribose 5-phosphate.

siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.name: Fructose and mannose metabolism
siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.description: The metabolic processes involved in the conversion and breakdown of fructose and mannose into glycolytic intermediates.

siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.name: Galactose metabolism
siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.description: The biochemical pathway responsible for converting galactose into glucose-1-phosphate to enter glycolysis.

siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.name: Ascorbate and aldarate metabolism
siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.description: The metabolic reactions involving the synthesis and degradation of vitamin C and various sugar acids.

siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.name: Pyruvate metabolism
siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.description: The metabolic hub connecting glycolysis to the citric acid cycle through the regulation of pyruvate levels.

siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.name: Pentose and glucuronate interconversions
siblings:carbohydrate metabolism.description: The metabolic interconversions between five-carbon sugars and sugar acids such as glucuronate.

siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.name: Nervous System Signal Transduction
siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.description: Signaling processes governing neuronal communication, synaptic transmission, and sensory transduction.

siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.name: Immune System Signal Transduction
siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.description: Intracellular signaling pathways involved in the activation and regulation of innate and adaptive immune responses.

siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.name: Respiratory System Signal Transduction
siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.description: Regulation of pulmonary function, including gas exchange signaling and surfactant production control.

siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.name: Digestive System Signal Transduction
siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.description: Signaling cascades controlling gastrointestinal motility, enzyme secretion, and nutrient absorption.

siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.name: Endocrine System Signal Transduction
siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.description: Communication pathways mediated by hormones to regulate systemic physiological functions and metabolism.

siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.name: Renal System Signal Transduction
siblings:organ system-specific signal tr.description: Pathways regulating ion transport, water reabsorption, and acid-base balance within the kidneys.

siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.name: Starch Metabolism
siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.description: The biological processes involved in the synthesis and degradation of starch, a storage polysaccharide in plants.

siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.name: Cellulose Metabolism
siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.description: Pathways responsible for the production and breakdown of cellulose, a primary structural component of plant cell walls.

siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.name: Chitin Metabolism
siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.description: The metabolic synthesis and enzymatic degradation of chitin, found in fungal cell walls and arthropod exoskeletons.

siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.name: Peptidoglycan Metabolism
siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.description: Processes involved in the assembly and remodeling of the bacterial cell wall polysaccharide-peptide complex.

siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.name: Glycosaminoglycan Metabolism
siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.description: The synthesis and degradation of long, unbranched polysaccharides such as heparin and hyaluronan in animal tissues.

siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.name: Hemicellulose Metabolism
siblings:polysaccharide metabolism.description: Metabolic pathways involving heteropolymers like xyloglucan and xylan that provide structural support in plants.

siblings:intracellular signal transducti.name: MAPK signaling pathway
siblings:intracellular signal transducti.description: A chain of proteins in the cell that communicates a signal from a receptor on the surface of the cell to the DNA in the nucleus.

siblings:intracellular signal transducti.name: JAK-STAT signaling pathway
siblings:intracellular signal transducti.description: Transmits information from extracellular chemical signals to the nucleus, resulting in the transcription of genes involved in immunity and proliferation.

siblings:intracellular signal transducti.name: PI3K-Akt signaling pathway
siblings:intracellular signal transducti.description: An intracellular signal transduction pathway that promotes metabolism, proliferation, cell survival, and growth.

siblings:intracellular signal transducti.name: TGF-beta signaling pathway
siblings:intracellular signal transducti.description: Regulates diverse cellular processes, including cell growth, differentiation, and development, via SMAD proteins.

siblings:intracellular signal transducti.name: NF-kappaB signaling pathway
siblings:intracellular signal transducti.description: A protein complex that controls transcription of DNA, cytokine production, and cell survival in response to stress and inflammation.

siblings:intracellular signal transducti.name: Wnt signaling pathway
siblings:intracellular signal transducti.description: A group of signal transduction pathways made of proteins that pass signals into a cell through cell surface receptors to regulate embryonic development.

siblings:intracellular signal transducti.name: Notch signaling pathway
siblings:intracellular signal transducti.description: A highly conserved cell signaling system that promotes neighbor-to-neighbor communication and regulates cell-fate determination.

siblings:second messenger signaling.name: Cyclic AMP (cAMP) Signaling
siblings:second messenger signaling.description: Involves the conversion of ATP to cAMP by adenylyl cyclase, primarily activating protein kinase A (PKA).

siblings:second messenger signaling.name: Cyclic GMP (cGMP) Signaling
siblings:second messenger signaling.description: Utilizes cGMP generated by guanylyl cyclases to activate protein kinase G (PKG) and regulate ion channels.

siblings:second messenger signaling.name: Inositol Triphosphate (IP3) Signaling
siblings:second messenger signaling.description: A lipid-derived pathway where IP3 binds to receptors on the endoplasmic reticulum to mobilize intracellular calcium stores.

siblings:second messenger signaling.name: Diacylglycerol (DAG) Signaling
siblings:second messenger signaling.description: A membrane-bound signaling pathway that activates protein kinase C (PKC) in response to phospholipase C activity.

siblings:second messenger signaling.name: Nitric Oxide (NO) Signaling
siblings:second messenger signaling.description: A gaseous signaling pathway where NO diffuses across membranes to activate soluble guanylyl cyclase.

siblings:second messenger signaling.name: Arachidonic Acid Signaling
siblings:second messenger signaling.description: Involves the release of arachidonic acid from phospholipids, serving as a precursor for eicosanoids like prostaglandins.

siblings:second messenger signaling.name: Phosphatidylinositol (3,4,5)-trisphosphate (PIP3) Signaling
