siblings:second messenger signaling.description: Produced by PI3K, PIP3 acts as a docking site for signaling proteins containing PH domains, such as Akt.

siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.name: Eicosanoid Signaling
siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.description: Signaling mediated by bioactive lipids derived from arachidonic acid, including prostaglandins, leukotrienes, and thromboxanes.

siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.name: Sphingolipid Signaling
siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.description: Pathways involving molecules such as ceramide, sphingosine, and sphingosine-1-phosphate that regulate cell survival and apoptosis.

siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.name: Endocannabinoid Signaling
siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.description: A lipid-based retrograde signaling system that utilizes anandamide and 2-arachidonoylglycerol to regulate neurotransmission.

siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.name: Lysophospholipid Signaling
siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.description: Signaling through G protein-coupled receptors by lysophosphatidic acid (LPA) and sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P).

siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.name: Platelet-Activating Factor (PAF) Signaling
siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.description: A pathway mediated by a potent phospholipid that triggers inflammatory responses and platelet aggregation.

siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.name: Diacylglycerol (DAG) Signaling
siblings:lipid-mediated signaling.description: The signaling branch focused on the activation of Protein Kinase C and other targets by the membrane-bound second messenger DAG.

siblings:mrna processing.name: 5' Capping
siblings:mrna processing.description: The addition of a 7-methylguanosine cap to the 5' end of the pre-mRNA transcript to protect it from degradation.

siblings:mrna processing.name: 3' Cleavage and Polyadenylation
siblings:mrna processing.description: The process of cleaving the pre-mRNA and adding a poly(A) tail to the 3' end to enhance stability and translation.

siblings:mrna processing.name: RNA Editing
siblings:mrna processing.description: The post-transcriptional alteration of specific nucleotide sequences within the mRNA molecule.

siblings:mrna processing.name: mRNA Export
siblings:mrna processing.description: The transport of mature mRNA from the nucleus to the cytoplasm through the nuclear pore complex.

siblings:mrna processing.name: mRNA Degradation
siblings:mrna processing.description: The regulated breakdown of mRNA molecules to control the duration and level of gene expression.

siblings:mrna processing.name: RNA Base Modification
siblings:mrna processing.description: The enzymatic modification of RNA bases, such as N6-methyladenosine methylation, which influences mRNA metabolism.

siblings:mrna processing.name: mRNA Surveillance
siblings:mrna processing.description: The quality control mechanisms that detect and degrade defective mRNA transcripts.

siblings:nutrient sensing.name: AMPK Signaling
siblings:nutrient sensing.description: A central energy sensor that monitors cellular AMP/ATP ratios to activate catabolic pathways during low energy states.

siblings:nutrient sensing.name: Insulin/IGF-1 Signaling
siblings:nutrient sensing.description: Senses extracellular glucose and growth factor availability to regulate nutrient uptake and anabolic metabolism.

siblings:nutrient sensing.name: Sirtuin Signaling
siblings:nutrient sensing.description: A family of NAD+-dependent deacetylases that sense cellular redox status and regulate metabolic homeostasis and aging.

siblings:nutrient sensing.name: GCN2 Pathway
siblings:nutrient sensing.description: Senses amino acid deficiency by detecting uncharged tRNAs and initiating the integrated stress response.

siblings:nutrient sensing.name: Hexosamine Biosynthetic Pathway
siblings:nutrient sensing.description: Integrates glucose, amino acid, and lipid metabolism to regulate protein function via O-GlcNAcylation.

siblings:nutrient sensing.name: PPAR Signaling
siblings:nutrient sensing.description: Nuclear receptors that sense the levels of fatty acids and their derivatives to regulate lipid and glucose metabolism.

siblings:growth factor signaling.name: PI3K-Akt Signaling
siblings:growth factor signaling.description: A primary intracellular pathway regulating the cell cycle, protein synthesis, and cell growth in response to growth factor receptor activation.

siblings:growth factor signaling.name: MAPK/ERK Signaling
siblings:growth factor signaling.description: A signal transduction cascade that couples extracellular growth factor signals to nuclear gene expression to regulate proliferation and differentiation.

siblings:growth factor signaling.name: JAK/STAT Signaling
siblings:growth factor signaling.description: A pathway that transfers signals from growth factor and cytokine receptors directly to the nucleus to regulate growth-related genes.

siblings:growth factor signaling.name: TGF-beta Signaling
siblings:growth factor signaling.description: Controls diverse biological processes including cell growth, differentiation, and development through the activation of Smad protein complexes.

siblings:growth factor signaling.name: Insulin/IGF-1 Signaling
siblings:growth factor signaling.description: An evolutionarily conserved pathway that acts as a major regulator of glucose metabolism, systemic growth, and longevity.

siblings:growth factor signaling.name: VEGF Signaling
siblings:growth factor signaling.description: A specialized growth factor pathway that coordinates the growth of new blood vessels and regulates vascular permeability.

siblings:growth factor signaling.name: EGF/EGFR Signaling
siblings:growth factor signaling.description: Triggers a cascade of events that promotes cell growth, proliferation, and survival in epithelial and other cell types.

siblings:small molecule signaling.name: Nucleotide Signaling
siblings:small molecule signaling.description: Signaling pathways utilizing molecules such as cAMP, cGMP, and ATP as secondary messengers or extracellular ligands.

siblings:small molecule signaling.name: Gasotransmitter Signaling
siblings:small molecule signaling.description: Signaling mediated by small gaseous molecules like nitric oxide, carbon monoxide, and hydrogen sulfide that diffuse across cell membranes.

siblings:small molecule signaling.name: Amino Acid-Mediated Signaling
siblings:small molecule signaling.description: Communication involving amino acids such as glutamate, GABA, and glycine, primarily acting as neurotransmitters or metabolic signals.

siblings:small molecule signaling.name: Calcium-Mediated Signaling
siblings:small molecule signaling.description: Intracellular signaling processes triggered by the movement and binding of calcium ions as a second messenger.

siblings:small molecule signaling.name: Biogenic Amine Signaling
siblings:small molecule signaling.description: Signaling involving small nitrogenous compounds such as serotonin, dopamine, histamine, and norepinephrine.

siblings:small molecule signaling.name: Redox Signaling
siblings:small molecule signaling.description: The use of reactive oxygen species or other small oxidant/reductant molecules to modulate cellular protein function and gene expression.

siblings:chemical signaling.name: Peptide and Protein Signaling
siblings:chemical signaling.description: Communication involving secreted peptides or proteins such as insulin, growth factors, and cytokines.

siblings:chemical signaling.name: Lipid Signaling
siblings:chemical signaling.description: Signaling mediated by hydrophobic lipid molecules including eicosanoids, sphingolipids, and fatty acids.

siblings:chemical signaling.name: Gasotransmitter Signaling
siblings:chemical signaling.description: The use of small gaseous molecules like nitric oxide and carbon monoxide to transmit biological signals.

siblings:chemical signaling.name: Nucleotide and Nucleoside Signaling
siblings:chemical signaling.description: Extracellular signaling using molecules like ATP, ADP, and adenosine via purinergic receptors.

siblings:chemical signaling.name: Amino Acid Signaling
siblings:chemical signaling.description: Signaling mediated by amino acids such as glutamate, aspartate, and GABA, particularly in synaptic transmission.

siblings:chemical signaling.name: Steroid Hormone Signaling
siblings:chemical signaling.description: Communication via cholesterol-derived hormones that typically modulate gene expression through nuclear receptors.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.name: Autophagic-Lysosomal Degradation
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.description: The process where ubiquitinated proteins or damaged organelles are sequestered into autophagosomes for degradation in lysosomes.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.name: Endocytic Pathway Degradation
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.description: Ubiquitin-mediated internalization of cell surface receptors and their subsequent trafficking to lysosomes for degradation.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.name: Mitophagy
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.description: A selective autophagic process that targets ubiquitinated mitochondria for degradation to maintain cellular homeostasis.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.name: Endoplasmic Reticulum-Associated Degradation (ERAD)
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.description: The targeting of misfolded ER proteins for ubiquitination and translocation to the cytoplasm for eventual clearance.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.name: Xenophagy
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.description: A specialized form of autophagy that uses ubiquitin tags to identify and degrade intracellular bacterial and viral pathogens.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.name: Pexophagy
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.description: The selective degradation of peroxisomes through the ubiquitin-dependent autophagic pathway in response to environmental changes.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.name: Ribophagy
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent protein deg.description: The targeted degradation of ribosomes via ubiquitin-dependent selective autophagy, often triggered by nutrient starvation.

siblings:rna processing.name: rRNA Processing
siblings:rna processing.description: The maturation of ribosomal RNA precursors into functional 18S, 5.8S, and 28S rRNA components.

siblings:rna processing.name: tRNA Processing
siblings:rna processing.description: The enzymatic modification and cleavage of precursor tRNA transcripts to produce mature transfer RNAs.

siblings:rna processing.name: snRNA Processing
siblings:rna processing.description: The maturation steps for small nuclear RNAs which are essential components of the spliceosome.

siblings:rna processing.name: snoRNA Processing
siblings:rna processing.description: The processing of small nucleolar RNAs that guide the chemical modification of rRNA and other RNAs.

siblings:rna processing.name: miRNA Processing
siblings:rna processing.description: The biogenesis of microRNAs from primary transcripts through Drosha and Dicer-mediated cleavage.

siblings:rna processing.name: siRNA Processing
siblings:rna processing.description: The processing of long double-stranded RNA into small interfering RNAs by the Dicer enzyme.

siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.name: Ubiquitination
siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.description: The enzymatic process involving E1, E2, and E3 enzymes that covalently attaches ubiquitin moieties to target proteins.

siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.name: Deubiquitination
siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.description: The removal of ubiquitin molecules from substrates by deubiquitinating enzymes (DUBs) to regulate protein stability.

siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.name: Proteasome Assembly
siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.description: The regulated multi-step formation of the 26S proteasome complex from its 20S core and 19S regulatory subunits.

siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.name: Endoplasmic Reticulum-Associated Degradation (ERAD)
siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.description: The pathway identifying misfolded proteins in the ER and translocating them to the cytosol for UPS-mediated destruction.

siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.name: Ubiquitin Recycling
siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.description: The disassembly of polyubiquitin chains into free ubiquitin monomers to maintain the cellular pool of available ubiquitin.

siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.name: E3 Ligase-Mediated Substrate Recognition
siblings:ubiquitin-proteasome system.description: The specific identification and binding of target proteins via degron motifs to ensure selective ubiquitination.

siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.name: PI3K/SGK Signaling
siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.description: Activation of serum/glucocorticoid regulated kinases involved in ion transport and cell survival.

siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.name: PI3K/PDK1 Signaling
siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.description: Activation of PDK1 which regulates multiple AGC kinase family members independently of Akt.

siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.name: PI3K/Tec Kinase Signaling
siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.description: Recruitment of Tec family kinases like BTK for calcium signaling and B-cell activation.

siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.name: PI3K/Rac1/Cdc42 Signaling
siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.description: Regulation of Rho-family GTPases governing cytoskeletal dynamics and cell motility.

siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.name: PI3K/mTOR Signaling
siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.description: Coordination of cellular growth and protein synthesis via mTOR complex 1 and 2.

siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.name: PI3K/PKC Signaling
siblings:phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (.description: Activation of protein kinase C isoforms to mediate varied cellular functions including proliferation.

siblings:translation.name: Mitochondrial Translation
siblings:translation.description: The process of protein synthesis specifically occurring within mitochondria using organelle-specific ribosomes.

siblings:translation.name: Translation Initiation
siblings:translation.description: The assembly of the translation machinery at the start codon of an mRNA molecule.

siblings:translation.name: Translation Elongation
siblings:translation.description: The stepwise addition of amino acids to a growing polypeptide chain guided by mRNA codons.

siblings:translation.name: Translation Termination
siblings:translation.description: The release of the completed polypeptide chain and dissociation of the ribosome upon reaching a stop codon.

siblings:translation.name: Ribosome Recycling
siblings:translation.description: The process of splitting the post-termination ribosomal complex into subunits for subsequent rounds of translation.

siblings:translation.name: Viral mRNA Translation
siblings:translation.description: Pathways used by viral pathogens to hijack host translation machinery for the synthesis of viral proteins.

siblings:primary metabolism.name: Lipid Metabolism
siblings:primary metabolism.description: Biochemical pathways involved in the synthesis and degradation of lipids and fatty acids.

siblings:primary metabolism.name: Amino Acid Metabolism
siblings:primary metabolism.description: Chemical processes governing the synthesis and breakdown of amino acids.

siblings:primary metabolism.name: Nucleotide Metabolism
siblings:primary metabolism.description: Pathways responsible for the production and recycling of purines and pyrimidines.

siblings:primary metabolism.name: Energy Metabolism
siblings:primary metabolism.description: Processes such as oxidative phosphorylation and photosynthesis that generate and store cellular energy.

siblings:primary metabolism.name: Metabolism of Cofactors and Vitamins
siblings:primary metabolism.description: Synthesis and maintenance of organic molecules required as essential enzymatic helpers.

siblings:primary metabolism.name: Glycan Biosynthesis and Metabolism
siblings:primary metabolism.description: Processes involved in the construction and modification of complex carbohydrates and sugar chains.

siblings:metabolism.name: Secondary Metabolism
siblings:metabolism.description: Pathways producing specialized compounds not directly involved in growth or reproduction, often used for defense or signaling.

siblings:metabolism.name: Xenobiotic Metabolism
siblings:metabolism.description: The set of metabolic pathways that modify the chemical structure of foreign substances, such as drugs and environmental pollutants.

siblings:metabolism.name: Energy Metabolism
siblings:metabolism.description: Biochemical processes focused on the conversion of nutrients into chemical energy in the form of ATP.

siblings:metabolism.name: Lipid Metabolism
siblings:metabolism.description: The synthesis and degradation of lipids, including fatty acids, triglycerides, and cholesterol.

siblings:metabolism.name: Carbohydrate Metabolism
siblings:metabolism.description: The biochemical processes responsible for the formation, breakdown, and interconversion of sugars and starches.

siblings:metabolism.name: Amino Acid Metabolism
siblings:metabolism.description: The metabolic pathways involved in the biosynthesis and catabolism of proteins and nitrogenous compounds.

siblings:metabolism.name: Nucleotide Metabolism
siblings:metabolism.description: The pathways for the synthesis and degradation of purines and pyrimidines required for DNA and RNA.

siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.name: Phospholipase C (PLC) Signaling
siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.description: The hydrolysis of phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate into the second messengers inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate and diacylglycerol.

siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.name: Phosphoinositide 4-Kinase (PI4K) Signaling
siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.description: The production of phosphatidylinositol 4-phosphate, a key regulatory lipid and precursor for higher-order phosphoinositides.

siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.name: Phosphoinositide 5-Kinase (PIP5K) Signaling
siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.description: The enzymatic synthesis of phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate from phosphatidylinositol 4-phosphate.

siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.name: PTEN Phosphatase Signaling
siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.description: The negative regulation of the PI3K pathway through the dephosphorylation of phosphatidylinositol 3,4,5-trisphosphate back to the 4,5-bisphosphate form.

siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.name: SHIP Phosphatase Signaling
siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.description: The conversion of phosphatidylinositol 3,4,5-trisphosphate into phosphatidylinositol 3,4-bisphosphate by SH2-containing inositol 5-phosphatases.

siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.name: Inositol Phosphate Metabolism
siblings:phosphoinositide signaling.description: The set of enzymatic reactions that interconvert various inositol phosphate isomers following the action of phospholipases.

siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.name: Cellular Respiration
siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.description: The metabolic process by which cells convert chemical energy from nutrients into adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and release waste products.

siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.name: Photosynthesis
siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.description: The biological process of converting light energy into chemical energy, primarily used by autotrophs to synthesize organic compounds.

siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.name: Oxidative Phosphorylation
siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.description: The metabolic pathway where cells use enzymes to oxidize nutrients, thereby releasing energy used to produce ATP within the mitochondria.

siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.name: Thermogenesis
siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.description: The process of heat production in organisms, often involving the uncoupling of electron transport from ATP synthesis to maintain body temperature.

siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.name: Electron Transport Chain
siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.description: A series of protein complexes and other molecules that transfer electrons through redox reactions to create a proton gradient for energy production.

siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.name: ATP Synthesis
siblings:metabolism & bioenergetics.description: The enzymatic production of ATP from ADP and inorganic phosphate, typically driven by the movement of protons across a membrane.

siblings:specialized signaling pathways.name: Synaptic Signaling
siblings:specialized signaling pathways.description: Communication between neurons via neurotransmitters at specialized junctions.

siblings:specialized signaling pathways.name: Phototransduction
siblings:specialized signaling pathways.description: The conversion of light stimuli into electrical signals within sensory cells.

siblings:specialized signaling pathways.name: Mechanotransduction
siblings:specialized signaling pathways.description: The translation of mechanical forces into biochemical signals to regulate cellular responses.

siblings:specialized signaling pathways.name: Immune Response Signaling
siblings:specialized signaling pathways.description: Specialized signal cascades that coordinate cellular defenses against pathogens and injury.

siblings:specialized signaling pathways.name: Hormonal Signaling
siblings:specialized signaling pathways.description: Systemic coordination of physiological processes through endocrine-derived chemical messengers.

siblings:specialized signaling pathways.name: Circadian Rhythm Signaling
siblings:specialized signaling pathways.description: Molecular pathways that synchronize biological activities with the 24-hour solar cycle.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.name: Protein Ubiquitination
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.description: The enzymatic cascade involving E1, E2, and E3 ligases that tags target proteins with ubiquitin molecules.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.name: Deubiquitination
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.description: The removal of ubiquitin from substrates by deubiquitinating enzymes to regulate signaling or recycle ubiquitin.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.name: Ubiquitin-dependent Endocytosis
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.description: A pathway where ubiquitin tags on membrane proteins trigger their internalization and delivery to lysosomes for degradation.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.name: Selective Autophagy
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.description: A process in which ubiquitinated proteins or organelles are sequestered into autophagosomes for lysosomal destruction.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.name: ER-associated degradation (ERAD)
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.description: The specific targeting of misfolded endoplasmic reticulum proteins for retrotranslocation and ubiquitin-dependent destruction.

siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.name: N-end rule pathway
siblings:ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis.description: A proteolytic system that relates the in vivo half-life of a protein to the identity of its N-terminal amino acid.

siblings:small molecule transport.name: Carbohydrate Transport
siblings:small molecule transport.description: The translocation of sugars such as glucose, fructose, and galactose across biological membranes.

siblings:small molecule transport.name: Amino Acid Transport
siblings:small molecule transport.description: The movement of amino acids into or out of cells, often mediated by specific symporters or antiporters.

siblings:small molecule transport.name: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Transport
siblings:small molecule transport.description: The transport of nitrogenous bases and their derivatives, essential for DNA and RNA synthesis.

siblings:small molecule transport.name: Lipid Transport
siblings:small molecule transport.description: The process of moving fatty acids, sterols, and other lipophilic molecules across cellular barriers.

siblings:small molecule transport.name: Vitamin and Cofactor Transport
siblings:small molecule transport.description: The specialized movement of essential micronutrients and enzymatic precursors across membranes.

siblings:small molecule transport.name: Water Transport
siblings:small molecule transport.description: The movement of water molecules, primarily facilitated by specialized channels known as aquaporins.

siblings:small molecule transport.name: Organic Anion and Cation Transport
siblings:small molecule transport.description: The transport of a wide variety of organic metabolites, signaling molecules, and xenobiotics.

siblings:energy homeostasis.name: Appetite and Satiety Regulation
siblings:energy homeostasis.description: The hypothalamic control of food intake and energy expenditure through orexigenic and anorexigenic signaling.

siblings:energy homeostasis.name: Glucose Homeostasis
siblings:energy homeostasis.description: The systematic regulation of blood glucose levels through insulin secretion and glucagon-mediated hepatic glucose production.

siblings:energy homeostasis.name: Lipid Metabolism
siblings:energy homeostasis.description: The processes of lipogenesis, lipolysis, and fatty acid oxidation that manage the storage and utilization of triglycerides.

siblings:energy homeostasis.name: Glycogen Metabolism
siblings:energy homeostasis.description: The balanced pathways of glycogenesis and glycogenolysis used to store and mobilize glucose in the liver and skeletal muscle.

siblings:energy homeostasis.name: Gluconeogenesis
siblings:energy homeostasis.description: The metabolic pathway that synthesizes glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors to maintain energy supply during fasting.

siblings:energy homeostasis.name: Mitochondrial Oxidative Phosphorylation
siblings:energy homeostasis.description: The cellular process of ATP production using energy released by the oxidation of nutrients within the electron transport chain.

siblings:protein misfolding.name: ER-associated degradation (ERAD)
siblings:protein misfolding.description: A cellular pathway that targets misfolded proteins in the endoplasmic reticulum for ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation.

siblings:protein misfolding.name: Chaperone-mediated refolding
siblings:protein misfolding.description: A process where molecular chaperones bind to misfolded proteins to assist them in achieving their native three-dimensional structure.

siblings:protein misfolding.name: Amyloid formation
siblings:protein misfolding.description: The specific structural transition of misfolded proteins into highly organized, insoluble cross-beta sheet fibrillar structures.

siblings:protein misfolding.name: Aggresome formation
siblings:protein misfolding.description: The sequestration of misfolded proteins and small aggregates into a large perinuclear structure near the microtubule-organizing center.

siblings:protein misfolding.name: Unfolded Protein Response (UPR)
siblings:protein misfolding.description: A cellular stress response pathway activated by the accumulation of misfolded proteins in the lumen of the endoplasmic reticulum.

siblings:protein misfolding.name: Proteasomal degradation
siblings:protein misfolding.description: The tagging and subsequent proteolysis of misfolded proteins by the ubiquitin-proteasome system to maintain proteostasis.

siblings:energy metabolism.name: Glycolysis
siblings:energy metabolism.description: A metabolic pathway that converts glucose into pyruvate, producing a small amount of ATP and NADH.

siblings:energy metabolism.name: Citric Acid Cycle
siblings:energy metabolism.description: A series of reactions used by aerobic organisms to release stored energy through the oxidation of acetyl-CoA derived from carbohydrates, fats, and proteins.

siblings:energy metabolism.name: Pentose Phosphate Pathway
siblings:energy metabolism.description: A process that generates NADPH and five-carbon sugars, serving as an alternative pathway for glucose oxidation.

siblings:energy metabolism.name: Beta-Oxidation
siblings:energy metabolism.description: The catabolic process by which fatty acid molecules are broken down in the mitochondria to generate acetyl-CoA for the Citric Acid Cycle.

siblings:energy metabolism.name: Gluconeogenesis
siblings:energy metabolism.description: The synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors, such as lactate, glycerol, and glucogenic amino acids.

siblings:energy metabolism.name: Glycogenolysis
siblings:energy metabolism.description: The primary pathway for the breakdown of glycogen into glucose-6-phosphate or glucose to maintain blood sugar levels.

siblings:energy metabolism.name: Carbon Fixation
siblings:energy metabolism.description: The process by which inorganic carbon is converted to organic compounds by living organisms, typically through the Calvin cycle.

siblings:transcriptional regulation.name: Transcriptional Activation
siblings:transcriptional regulation.description: The process of increasing the rate of gene transcription by the binding of activator proteins to specific DNA sequences.

siblings:transcriptional regulation.name: Transcriptional Initiation
siblings:transcriptional regulation.description: The initial stage of transcription involving the assembly of RNA polymerase and transcription factors at the promoter.

siblings:transcriptional regulation.name: Transcriptional Elongation
siblings:transcriptional regulation.description: The stage where RNA polymerase travels along the DNA template to synthesize a complementary RNA strand.

siblings:transcriptional regulation.name: Transcriptional Termination
siblings:transcriptional regulation.description: The final stage of transcription where the RNA polymerase and the nascent RNA transcript are released from the DNA.

siblings:transcriptional regulation.name: Transcriptional Pausing
siblings:transcriptional regulation.description: A regulatory mechanism where RNA polymerase halts at specific sites during elongation to coordinate gene expression.

siblings:transcriptional regulation.name: Transcriptional Interference
siblings:transcriptional regulation.description: The direct suppressive influence of one transcription process on another in cis, such as through promoter competition.

siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.name: Nuclear Export
siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.description: The process of transporting proteins and RNA molecules from the nucleoplasm to the cytoplasm through nuclear pore complexes.

siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.name: mRNA Export
siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.description: The specialized pathway for the transport of mature messenger RNA molecules to the cytoplasm for translation.

siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.name: tRNA Export
siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.description: The regulated movement of mature transfer RNAs out of the nucleus, typically mediated by specific exportins like XPO5.

siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.name: snRNA Export
siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.description: The export of small nuclear RNAs to the cytoplasm for further processing and assembly into ribonucleoprotein particles.

siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.name: rRNA Export
siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.description: The translocation of ribosomal subunits from the nucleolus to the cytoplasm via the nuclear pore complex.

siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.name: Nuclear Pore Complex Assembly
siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.description: The structural process of constructing the large proteinaceous channels that span the nuclear envelope to facilitate transport.

siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.name: Ran-GTPase Cycle
siblings:nucleocytoplasmic transport.description: The regulatory mechanism involving Ran protein transitions between GTP-bound and GDP-bound states to provide energy and directionality to transport.

siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.name: ATP Synthesis
siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.description: The biochemical process where ATP synthase utilizes the electrochemical proton gradient to convert ADP and inorganic phosphate into ATP.

siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.name: Respiratory Uncoupling
siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.description: The process by which the proton gradient is dissipated as heat instead of being used for ATP synthesis, often mediated by uncoupling proteins.

siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.name: Proton Translocation
siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.description: The movement of protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane to establish the electrochemical gradient required for chemiosmosis.

siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.name: Supercomplex Assembly
siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.description: The structural organization of respiratory chain complexes into higher-order respirasomes to optimize electron transfer efficiency.

siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.name: Chemiosmotic Coupling
siblings:oxidative phosphorylation.description: The mechanism that links the energy released from the electron transport chain to the phosphorylation of ADP.

siblings:metabolic regulation.name: Glucose Homeostasis
siblings:metabolic regulation.description: The regulation of blood sugar levels through the coordinated actions of insulin, glucagon, and other hormones.

siblings:metabolic regulation.name: Lipid Homeostasis
siblings:metabolic regulation.description: The management of fatty acid and cholesterol synthesis, storage, and mobilization within the body.

siblings:metabolic regulation.name: Amino Acid Homeostasis
siblings:metabolic regulation.description: Maintenance of the pool of essential and non-essential amino acids through synthesis and degradation.

siblings:metabolic regulation.name: Redox Homeostasis
siblings:metabolic regulation.description: The balancing of oxidative and reductive chemical reactions to maintain cellular stability and prevent damage.

siblings:metabolic regulation.name: Nucleotide Homeostasis
siblings:metabolic regulation.description: The control of purine and pyrimidine synthesis and breakdown to ensure availability for DNA and RNA production.

siblings:metabolic regulation.name: Mineral Homeostasis
siblings:metabolic regulation.description: The metabolic regulation of essential ions such as calcium, phosphate, and magnesium.

siblings:metabolic regulation.name: pH Homeostasis
siblings:metabolic regulation.description: The metabolic management of acid-base balance to maintain physiological hydrogen ion concentrations.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Cytoskeleton Organization
siblings:cellular component organization.description: Processes that result in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of the cytoskeleton.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Plasma Membrane Organization
siblings:cellular component organization.description: Processes that result in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of the plasma membrane.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Cell Wall Organization
siblings:cellular component organization.description: The assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of a cell wall.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Extracellular Matrix Organization
siblings:cellular component organization.description: Processes that result in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of an extracellular matrix.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Protein Complex Assembly
siblings:cellular component organization.description: The aggregation, arrangement, and bonding together of a set of macromolecules to form a protein complex.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: External Encapsulating Structure Organization
siblings:cellular component organization.description: Processes that result in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of a structure that lies outside the plasma membrane.

siblings:apoptosis signaling.name: Intrinsic (Mitochondrial) Pathway
siblings:apoptosis signaling.description: A pathway activated by internal stimuli such as DNA damage or oxidative stress, leading to mitochondrial membrane permeabilization and cytochrome c release.

siblings:apoptosis signaling.name: Extrinsic (Death Receptor) Pathway
siblings:apoptosis signaling.description: Triggered by extracellular signal molecules binding to cell surface death receptors like TNF-R1 or Fas.

siblings:apoptosis signaling.name: Perforin/Granzyme Pathway
siblings:apoptosis signaling.description: Utilized by cytotoxic T lymphocytes and natural killer cells to deliver proteases that directly activate apoptotic processes in target cells.

siblings:apoptosis signaling.name: ER Stress-induced Apoptosis
siblings:apoptosis signaling.description: Activated when the endoplasmic reticulum undergoes prolonged stress, triggering the unfolded protein response and pro-apoptotic signaling.

siblings:apoptosis signaling.name: Caspase Signaling Cascade
siblings:apoptosis signaling.description: The central proteolytic mechanism involving initiator and executioner caspases that systematically dismantles the cell.

siblings:apoptosis signaling.name: Anoikis
siblings:apoptosis signaling.description: A specialized form of programmed cell death induced by the loss of cell-extracellular matrix interaction.

siblings:p53 signaling.name: p53-Dependent Cell Cycle Arrest
siblings:p53 signaling.description: The process by which p53 activates p21 (CDKN1A) to inhibit cyclin-dependent kinases, halting the cell cycle for DNA repair.

siblings:p53 signaling.name: p53-Dependent Cellular Senescence
siblings:p53 signaling.description: A permanent state of cell cycle withdrawal induced by p53 in response to oncogenic stress or telomere attrition.

siblings:p53 signaling.name: p53-Mediated DNA Repair
siblings:p53 signaling.description: Transcriptional upregulation of genes such as GADD45 and RRM2B to maintain genomic integrity after damage.

siblings:p53 signaling.name: p53-Regulated Metabolism
siblings:p53 signaling.description: Modulation of glycolysis, oxidative phosphorylation, and antioxidant response through targets like TIGAR and SCO2.

siblings:p53 signaling.name: p53-Dependent Autophagy
siblings:p53 signaling.description: The induction or regulation of self-digestive catabolic processes to manage cellular stress or nutrient deprivation.

siblings:p53 signaling.name: p53-Mediated Ferroptosis
siblings:p53 signaling.description: An iron-dependent form of regulated cell death promoted by p53 through the repression of cystine/glutamate antiporters.

siblings:p53 signaling.name: p53-Dependent Inhibition of Angiogenesis
siblings:p53 signaling.description: The suppression of new blood vessel formation via the downregulation of pro-angiogenic factors like VEGF.

siblings:apoptosis.name: Intrinsic Apoptotic Signaling Pathway
siblings:apoptosis.description: A pathway initiated by internal cellular stress signals that leads to mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization.

siblings:apoptosis.name: Extrinsic Apoptotic Signaling Pathway
siblings:apoptosis.description: A pathway initiated by the binding of extracellular death ligands to cell surface death receptors.

siblings:apoptosis.name: Execution Phase of Apoptosis
siblings:apoptosis.description: The final stage of apoptosis characterized by the activation of executioner caspases and systemic cellular degradation.

siblings:apoptosis.name: Apoptotic DNA Fragmentation
siblings:apoptosis.description: The biochemical process where nuclear DNA is cleaved into nucleosomal fragments by endonucleases.

siblings:apoptosis.name: Caspase Activation
siblings:apoptosis.description: The sequential proteolytic cleavage of pro-caspases into active enzymes that drive the apoptotic process.

siblings:apoptosis.name: Apoptotic Body Formation
siblings:apoptosis.description: The process of cell shrinkage and membrane blebbing that results in the formation of discrete membrane-bound vesicles.

siblings:apoptosis.name: Apoptotic Cell Clearance
siblings:apoptosis.description: The recognition and engulfment of apoptotic cells or fragments by phagocytes, also known as efferocytosis.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Cellular Component Biogenesis
siblings:cellular component organization.description: The process that results in the biosynthesis of constituent macromolecules and their assembly into a cellular component.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Cellular Component Disassembly
siblings:cellular component organization.description: The process that results in the breakdown of a cellular component into its constituent parts.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Protein Complex Subunit Organization
siblings:cellular component organization.description: A process that results in the formation, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of a protein-containing complex.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: External Encapsulating Structure Organization
siblings:cellular component organization.description: A process that results in the assembly, arrangement of constituent parts, or disassembly of an external encapsulating structure.

siblings:cellular component organization.name: Cellular Component Maintenance
siblings:cellular component organization.description: The process of keeping a cellular component in a functional state over time.

siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.name: Mitophagy
siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.description: The selective degradation of damaged or redundant mitochondria through the autophagic pathway.

siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.name: Mitochondrial Biogenesis
siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.description: The process by which cells increase mitochondrial mass and number in response to metabolic demand.

siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.name: Mitochondrial Fusion
siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.description: The physical merging of outer and inner mitochondrial membranes to exchange genetic material and proteins.

siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.name: Mitochondrial Fission
siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.description: The division of mitochondria into smaller units to facilitate transport and quality control.

siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.name: Mitochondrial Proteostasis
siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.description: The regulation of mitochondrial protein synthesis, folding, and degradation, including the unfolded protein response (UPRmt).

siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.name: Mitochondrial Calcium Homeostasis
siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.description: The regulation of calcium uptake and efflux to maintain metabolic signaling and prevent cell death.

siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.name: Mitochondrial Redox Homeostasis
siblings:mitochondrial homeostasis.description: The balance between the production and scavenging of reactive oxygen species within the organelle.

siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.name: Focal Adhesion Disassembly
siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.description: The controlled breakdown of focal adhesion complexes to facilitate cellular detachment and movement.

siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.name: Integrin Signaling
siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.description: The transmission of biochemical signals across the plasma membrane following integrin-ligand binding.

siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.name: Hemidesmosome Assembly
siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.description: The formation of stable, integrin-linked junctions that anchor cells to the underlying basement membrane.

siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.name: Integrin Cell Surface Interactions
siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.description: The initial engagement of cell-surface integrin receptors with extracellular matrix components or counter-receptors.

siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.name: Integrin-Mediated Cell Migration
siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.description: The coordinated process of cell translocation driven by the dynamic regulation of integrin-based adhesions.

siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.name: Activation of Integrins
siblings:integrin-mediated adhesion.description: The process by which integrins undergo conformational changes to increase their affinity for ligands.

siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.name: AIM2 Inflammasome Pathway
siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.description: A pathway where the sensor AIM2 binds cytosolic double-stranded DNA to trigger the assembly of the inflammasome and activation of caspase-1.

siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.name: DAI (ZBP1) Pathway
siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.description: Mediates the induction of type I interferons and other cytokines by sensing intracellular DNA through the DNA-dependent activator of IRFs.

siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.name: IFI16 Signaling Pathway
siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.description: A member of the PYHIN family that senses cytosolic and nuclear DNA to induce the expression of interferon-stimulated genes.

siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.name: RNA Polymerase III-RIG-I Pathway
siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.description: Converts cytosolic AT-rich DNA into 5'-triphosphate RNA, which is then recognized by the RIG-I receptor to trigger an antiviral response.

siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.name: DHX9/DHX36 Helicase Pathways
siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.description: DExD/H-box helicases that act as sensors for cytosolic DNA to activate IRF7 and NF-kappaB signaling in plasmacytoid dendritic cells.

siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.name: LRRFIP1 Signaling Pathway
siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.description: Recognizes double-stranded DNA and mediates the production of IFN-beta via a mechanism involving Beta-catenin.

siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.name: cGAS-mediated Signaling
siblings:cytosolic dna-sensing pathway.description: Though often coupled with STING, cGAS acts as the primary enzymatic sensor that produces cGAMP upon DNA binding to initiate downstream signaling.

siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.name: JUNQ (Juxtanuclear Quality Control compartment)
siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.description: A compartment for the sequestration of soluble ubiquitinated proteins destined for proteasomal degradation.

siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.name: IPOD (Insoluble Protein Deposit)
siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.description: A site for the sequestration of terminally misfolded, insoluble, or amyloidogenic proteins.

siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.name: ALIS (Aggresome-like induced structures)
siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.description: Transient cytoplasmic bodies containing ubiquitinated proteins that form in response to inflammatory or oxidative stress.

siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.name: Inclusion body formation
siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.description: General formation of dense protein aggregates often containing misfolded proteins, commonly observed in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.name: Nucleolar sequestration
siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.description: The temporary recruitment and immobilization of misfolded proteins within the nucleolus during proteotoxic stress.

siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.name: Proteasome storage granules
siblings:misfolded protein sequestration.description: Cytoplasmic structures where proteasomes and misfolded substrates are sequestered during periods of metabolic transition or starvation.

siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.name: Cell Junctions
siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.description: Specialized protein complexes that provide physical connections and communication channels between adjacent cells, including tight junctions and gap junctions.

siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.name: Extracellular Matrix Organization
siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.description: The assembly, degradation, and remodeling of the structural scaffold that provides biochemical and biomechanical support to surrounding cells.

siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.name: Intercellular Signaling
siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.description: The communication process where cells transmit and receive signals through direct physical contact or secreted molecules to coordinate biological activities.

siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.name: Transendothelial Migration
siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.description: The process by which cells, such as leukocytes, squeeze between or through endothelial cells to move from the bloodstream into tissues.

siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.name: Contact Inhibition
siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.description: A regulatory mechanism that functions to keep cells growing into a layer one cell thick, stopping movement or proliferation upon physical contact.

siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.name: Basement Membrane Assembly
siblings:cell-cell and cell-matrix inter.description: The formation of the specialized extracellular matrix layer that separates epithelial or endothelial cells from underlying connective tissue.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: ER-associated degradation (ERAD)
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: The pathway responsible for identifying, retrotranslocating, and targeting misfolded ER proteins for proteasomal degradation.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: Calnexin/Calreticulin Cycle
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: A chaperone-mediated process that ensures the proper folding and quality check of newly synthesized N-linked glycoproteins.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: ER-phagy
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: The selective autophagy of the endoplasmic reticulum used to clear large protein aggregates or surplus membrane components.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: ER Export and Cargo Selection
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: The process of sorting and packaging correctly folded proteins into COPII vesicles for transport to the Golgi apparatus.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: Protein N-glycosylation and Glycan Trimming
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: The enzymatic modification of proteins that acts as a molecular timer to determine the folding status and fate of the polypeptide.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.name: ER-to-Golgi Quality Control
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum quality c.description: Mechanisms that monitor protein maturation during trafficking, including the retrieval of escaped ER-resident proteins via the KDEL receptor.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.name: ER-associated degradation (ERAD)
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.description: A process that identifies terminally misfolded proteins in the ER and targets them for cytosolic proteasomal degradation.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.name: ER-stress-induced Apoptosis
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.description: A programmed cell death pathway triggered via caspase activation when ER stress is prolonged and homeostasis cannot be restored.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.name: ER-phagy
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.description: A selective form of autophagy that mediates the degradation of damaged or redundant endoplasmic reticulum components.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.name: Integrated Stress Response (ISR)
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.description: A signaling network that overlaps with UPR branches to regulate protein synthesis via eIF2-alpha phosphorylation.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.name: Calcium Homeostasis Regulation
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.description: Mechanisms that adjust luminal calcium concentrations to maintain ER chaperone activity and protein folding capacity.

siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.name: UFMylation
siblings:endoplasmic reticulum stress re.description: A post-translational modification system involving the UFM1 protein that protects cells from ER-stress-induced dysfunction.

siblings:spatial protein quality control.name: JUNQ (Juxtanuclear Quality Control) Formation
siblings:spatial protein quality control.description: The partitioning of soluble ubiquitinated proteins to a juxtanuclear compartment for proteasomal degradation or refolding.

siblings:spatial protein quality control.name: IPOD (Insoluble Protein Deposit) Formation
siblings:spatial protein quality control.description: The sequestration of terminally misfolded or amyloidogenic proteins into a perivacuolar compartment for long-term storage or autophagy.

siblings:spatial protein quality control.name: Asymmetric Aggregate Segregation
siblings:spatial protein quality control.description: The selective partitioning of damaged protein aggregates to a single daughter cell during mitosis to ensure the rejuvenation of the other.

siblings:spatial protein quality control.name: Nucleolar Protein Sequestration
siblings:spatial protein quality control.description: The transient storage of misfolded proteins within the nucleolus to prevent toxic interactions during proteotoxic stress.

siblings:spatial protein quality control.name: Aggrephagy
siblings:spatial protein quality control.description: The selective autophagic pathway responsible for the recognition and degradation of sequestered protein aggregates.

siblings:spatial protein quality control.name: INQ (Intranuclear Quality Control) Formation
siblings:spatial protein quality control.description: The deposition of misfolded nuclear or cytosolic proteins into a specific site within the nucleus for management.

siblings:spatial protein quality control.name: Stress Granule Assembly
siblings:spatial protein quality control.description: The formation of cytoplasmic ribonucleoprotein complexes to protect translation machinery and specific proteins during stress.

siblings:cell junction organization.name: Cell-Extracellular Matrix Junction Organization
siblings:cell junction organization.description: The assembly and maintenance of junctional complexes that anchor the cell to the extracellular matrix.

siblings:cell junction organization.name: Cell-Substrate Junction Organization
siblings:cell junction organization.description: The organizational processes surrounding junctions that connect cells to a non-cellular underlying substrate.

siblings:cell junction organization.name: Hemidesmosome Assembly
siblings:cell junction organization.description: The specific formation of stable adhesive complexes that link the intermediate filament cytoskeleton to the basement membrane.

siblings:cell junction organization.name: Focal Adhesion Assembly
siblings:cell junction organization.description: The dynamic organization of multi-protein structures connecting the actin cytoskeleton to the extracellular matrix via integrins.

siblings:cell junction organization.name: Integrin-mediated Cell-Matrix Adhesion
siblings:cell junction organization.description: The signaling and structural processes regulating the interaction between cell surface integrins and the surrounding matrix.

siblings:dna sensing.name: TLR9 Signaling Pathway
siblings:dna sensing.description: Recognition of unmethylated CpG DNA motifs within the endosomal compartment by Toll-like receptor 9.

siblings:dna sensing.name: AIM2 Inflammasome Pathway
siblings:dna sensing.description: Detection of cytosolic double-stranded DNA by the AIM2 protein, leading to the activation of caspase-1 and inflammation.

siblings:dna sensing.name: RNA Polymerase III-RIG-I Pathway
siblings:dna sensing.description: Conversion of AT-rich DNA into 5-prime-triphosphate RNA by RNA Polymerase III, which is then detected by the RIG-I receptor.

siblings:dna sensing.name: IFI16 Signaling Pathway
siblings:dna sensing.description: A nuclear and cytosolic sensing mechanism where IFI16 detects viral DNA to initiate interferon-beta production.

siblings:dna sensing.name: ZBP1/DAI Pathway
siblings:dna sensing.description: Sensing of cytosolic DNA or Z-form DNA by Z-DNA binding protein 1 to induce pro-inflammatory cytokines and necroptosis.

siblings:dna sensing.name: DHX9/DHX36 Signaling
siblings:dna sensing.description: DExD/H-box helicases that function as DNA sensors to induce type I interferon production in plasmacytoid dendritic cells.

siblings:cell-cell communication.name: Cell-cell adhesion molecules (CAMs)
siblings:cell-cell communication.description: Proteins on the cell surface involved in binding with other cells or the extracellular matrix to mediate cell-cell recognition.

siblings:cell-cell communication.name: Chemical synaptic transmission
siblings:cell-cell communication.description: The process by which signaling molecules called neurotransmitters are released by a neuron and activate receptors of another neuron.

siblings:cell-cell communication.name: Gap junction trafficking
siblings:cell-cell communication.description: The regulation, transport, and assembly of connexin proteins into channels that allow direct intercellular communication.

siblings:cell-cell communication.name: Neurexins and neuroligins
siblings:cell-cell communication.description: Cell surface molecules that interact across the synaptic cleft to facilitate the maturation and function of synapses.

siblings:cell-cell communication.name: Synaptic adhesion-like molecules
siblings:cell-cell communication.description: A family of adhesion molecules involved in the formation, maintenance, and plasticity of neuronal synaptic connections.

siblings:cell-cell communication.name: Intercellular signal transduction
siblings:cell-cell communication.description: The relay of molecular signals from a sender cell to a receiver cell to coordinate physiological responses.

siblings:nucleic acid sensing.name: RIG-I-like Receptor (RLR) Signaling
siblings:nucleic acid sensing.description: The detection of cytosolic double-stranded and 5'-triphosphate RNA by sensors such as RIG-I and MDA5.

siblings:nucleic acid sensing.name: Toll-like Receptor 3 (TLR3) Signaling
siblings:nucleic acid sensing.description: An endosomal pathway dedicated to the recognition of double-stranded RNA (dsRNA).

siblings:nucleic acid sensing.name: Toll-like Receptor 7/8 (TLR7/8) Signaling
siblings:nucleic acid sensing.description: The endosomal sensing of single-stranded RNA (ssRNA) typically derived from viruses or damaged cells.

siblings:nucleic acid sensing.name: RNA Polymerase III Pathway
siblings:nucleic acid sensing.description: A mechanism that transcribes cytosolic DNA into 5'-triphosphate RNA, which is subsequently detected by the RIG-I receptor.

siblings:nucleic acid sensing.name: OAS-RNase L Pathway
siblings:nucleic acid sensing.description: The activation of 2'-5' oligoadenylate synthetase by double-stranded RNA, leading to RNA degradation and inhibition of viral replication.

siblings:nucleic acid sensing.name: Protein Kinase R (PKR) Activation
siblings:nucleic acid sensing.description: A double-stranded RNA-dependent protein kinase pathway that inhibits translation as part of the antiviral response.

siblings:unfolded protein response.name: PERK-mediated translation attenuation
siblings:unfolded protein response.description: Phosphorylation of eIF2 alpha by PERK to reduce the overall protein synthesis rate and lower the ER load.

siblings:unfolded protein response.name: IRE1 alpha-mediated XBP1 splicing
siblings:unfolded protein response.description: The processing of XBP1 mRNA by activated IRE1 to produce a functional transcription factor for foldases and ERAD components.

siblings:unfolded protein response.name: ATF6 alpha-mediated proteolysis
siblings:unfolded protein response.description: The translocation of ATF6 to the Golgi for cleavage by S1P and S2P proteases, releasing its cytosolic transcription factor domain.

siblings:unfolded protein response.name: ER-associated degradation (ERAD)
siblings:unfolded protein response.description: The process of identifying, retro-translocating, and degrading misfolded ER proteins via the ubiquitin-proteasome system.

siblings:unfolded protein response.name: Regulated IRE1-dependent decay (RIDD)
siblings:unfolded protein response.description: The degradation of specific ER-localized mRNAs and microRNAs by IRE1 to further reduce protein influx into the ER.

siblings:unfolded protein response.name: ER stress-induced apoptosis
siblings:unfolded protein response.description: Signaling cascades, often involving CHOP and JNK, that trigger programmed cell death when ER stress is persistent or severe.

siblings:cellular response to stimulus.name: Cellular response to stress
siblings:cellular response to stimulus.description: A change in state or activity of a cell resulting from a disturbance such as oxidative stress or protein folding stress.

siblings:cellular response to stimulus.name: Cellular response to chemical stimulus
siblings:cellular response to stimulus.description: The process where a cell changes its state or activity due to the detection of a chemical substance.

siblings:cellular response to stimulus.name: Cellular response to external stimulus
siblings:cellular response to stimulus.description: A change in cell state or activity resulting from a stimulus originating outside the organism or cell.

siblings:cellular response to stimulus.name: Cellular response to endogenous stimulus
siblings:cellular response to stimulus.description: The process in which a cell changes its state or activity as a result of an internal signal like a hormone or cytokine.

siblings:cellular response to stimulus.name: Cellular response to DNA damage stimulus
siblings:cellular response to stimulus.description: A set of molecular signals and actions triggered by damage to the cellular DNA, often leading to repair or apoptosis.

siblings:cellular response to stimulus.name: Cellular response to heat
siblings:cellular response to stimulus.description: Specific cellular changes, such as the induction of heat shock proteins, in response to an increase in temperature.

siblings:rna export.name: tRNA Nuclear Export
siblings:rna export.description: The transport of mature transfer RNAs from the nucleus to the cytoplasm, primarily mediated by the nuclear export receptor Exportin-t.

siblings:rna export.name: rRNA Nuclear Export
siblings:rna export.description: The coordinated movement of ribosomal RNA subunits (40S and 60S) from the nucleolus through nuclear pore complexes to the cytoplasm.

siblings:rna export.name: snRNA Nuclear Export
siblings:rna export.description: The export of small nuclear RNAs, such as U1, U2, U4, and U5, which are transported to the cytoplasm for assembly into snRNP particles.

siblings:rna export.name: miRNA Nuclear Export
siblings:rna export.description: The translocation of precursor microRNAs (pre-miRNAs) from the nucleus to the cytoplasm, typically facilitated by the Exportin-5 protein.

siblings:rna export.name: lncRNA Nuclear Export
siblings:rna export.description: The regulated transport of long non-coding RNAs from the nucleus to specific cytoplasmic locations to perform regulatory or structural roles.

siblings:non-coding rna processing.name: rRNA Processing
siblings:non-coding rna processing.description: The series of steps through which a precursor ribosomal RNA transcript is converted into mature ribosomal RNA molecules.

siblings:non-coding rna processing.name: tRNA Processing
siblings:non-coding rna processing.description: The maturation of transfer RNA including 5' and 3' end cleavage, base modifications, and CCA tail addition.

siblings:non-coding rna processing.name: snRNA Processing
siblings:non-coding rna processing.description: The process of maturing small nuclear RNAs which are critical components of the spliceosome.

siblings:non-coding rna processing.name: snoRNA Processing
siblings:non-coding rna processing.description: The biogenesis and maturation of small nucleolar RNAs that guide chemical modifications of other RNAs.

siblings:non-coding rna processing.name: lncRNA Processing
siblings:non-coding rna processing.description: The post-transcriptional maturation of long non-coding RNAs including capping, splicing, and polyadenylation.

siblings:non-coding rna processing.name: Vault RNA Processing
siblings:non-coding rna processing.description: The biochemical pathways responsible for the maturation of RNAs associated with vault ribonucleoprotein complexes.

siblings:translational control.name: Translation Initiation Regulation
siblings:translational control.description: The process of controlling the assembly of the eukaryotic initiation factor complex and the recruitment of the 40S ribosomal subunit.

siblings:translational control.name: Translation Elongation Regulation
siblings:translational control.description: Mechanisms that modulate the rate of peptide chain extension, often through the phosphorylation of eukaryotic elongation factor 2.

siblings:translational control.name: Translation Termination Regulation
siblings:translational control.description: The control of polypeptide release and ribosome recycling, ensuring the proper completion of protein synthesis.

siblings:translational control.name: mRNA Sequestration
siblings:translational control.description: The localization of mRNA into stress granules or P-bodies to temporarily remove them from the active translation pool.

siblings:translational control.name: IRES-mediated Translation
siblings:translational control.description: An alternative initiation pathway where ribosomes are recruited to internal ribosome entry sites, bypassing standard cap-dependent mechanisms.

siblings:translational control.name: Ribosome Biogenesis Regulation
siblings:translational control.description: The control of the production and assembly of ribosomal RNA and proteins, defining the cell's total translational capacity.

siblings:translational control.name: uORF-mediated Regulation
siblings:translational control.description: The use of upstream open reading frames to modulate the translation efficiency of the primary downstream protein-coding sequence.

siblings:inflammatory signaling.name: Anti-inflammatory Signaling
siblings:inflammatory signaling.description: Signaling pathways that downregulate the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and suppress immune responses.

siblings:inflammatory signaling.name: Pro-resolving Signaling
siblings:inflammatory signaling.description: Active biochemical processes mediated by specialized mediators that facilitate the termination of inflammation and promote tissue repair.

siblings:inflammatory signaling.name: Feedback Inhibition Signaling
siblings:inflammatory signaling.description: Intrinsic cellular mechanisms, such as SOCS proteins, that act as negative feedback loops to attenuate ongoing inflammatory signals.

siblings:inflammatory signaling.name: Regulatory Signaling
siblings:inflammatory signaling.description: Signaling mediated by regulatory immune cells to modulate the magnitude of the immune response and prevent autoimmunity.

siblings:inflammatory signaling.name: Homeostatic Signaling
siblings:inflammatory signaling.description: Basal level signaling required for tissue maintenance and physiological immune surveillance without active inflammatory recruitment.

siblings:inflammatory signaling.name: Chronic Inflammatory Signaling
siblings:inflammatory signaling.description: Dysregulated, persistent signaling patterns that fail to resolve and lead to long-term tissue damage.

siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.name: Ubiquitin-Independent Proteasomal Protein Catabolic Process
siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.description: The degradation of specific proteins by the proteasome complex without the requirement for prior polyubiquitination.

siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.name: Endoplasmic Reticulum-Associated Degradation (ERAD)
siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.description: A pathway that targets misfolded proteins in the endoplasmic reticulum for retrotranslocation and degradation by the proteasome.

siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.name: Mitochondria-Associated Degradation (MAD)
siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.description: The process of extracting and degrading damaged or misfolded mitochondrial proteins via the cytosolic proteasome.

siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.name: Immunoproteasome-Mediated Protein Degradation
siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.description: Degradation of proteins by a specialized proteasome isoform to generate peptides optimized for MHC class I antigen presentation.

siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.name: Thymoproteasome-Mediated Protein Degradation
siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.description: Specialized proteasomal activity in cortical thymic epithelial cells essential for the positive selection of T-cells.

siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.name: Proteasome-Mediated Degradation of Nuclear Proteins
siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.description: Targeted proteolysis of transcription factors and nuclear proteins within the nucleus to regulate gene expression.

siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.name: Regulation of Proteasomal Protein Catabolic Process
siblings:proteasomal protein degradation.description: Any biological process that modulates the frequency, rate, or extent of protein degradation by the proteasome.

siblings:dna double-strand break repair.name: Homologous Recombination
siblings:dna double-strand break repair.description: A high-fidelity repair mechanism that uses a homologous DNA sequence, typically a sister chromatid, as a template for accurate repair.

siblings:dna double-strand break repair.name: Microhomology-Mediated End Joining
siblings:dna double-strand break repair.description: An error-prone repair pathway that utilizes short homologous sequences flanking a break to align and ligate DNA ends.

siblings:dna double-strand break repair.name: Single-Strand Annealing
siblings:dna double-strand break repair.description: A repair mechanism initiated by extensive end resection that anneals homologous repeat sequences, resulting in the loss of intervening DNA.

siblings:dna double-strand break repair.name: Break-Induced Replication
siblings:dna double-strand break repair.description: A specialized pathway for repairing one-ended double-strand breaks by invading a homologous template and replicating to the chromosome end.

siblings:dna double-strand break repair.name: Synthesis-Dependent Strand Annealing
siblings:dna double-strand break repair.description: A sub-pathway of homologous recombination that results in gene conversion without crossing over by displacing the extended strand.

siblings:small rna processing.name: siRNA Biogenesis
siblings:small rna processing.description: The enzymatic processing of long double-stranded RNAs into small interfering RNAs by Dicer or Dicer-like proteins.

siblings:small rna processing.name: piRNA Biogenesis
siblings:small rna processing.description: The formation of Piwi-interacting RNAs through primary biogenesis and the secondary amplification (ping-pong) cycle.

siblings:small rna processing.name: tsRNA Biogenesis
siblings:small rna processing.description: The cleavage of mature or precursor transfer RNAs into tRNA-derived small RNAs or fragments.

siblings:small rna processing.name: snoRNA Processing
siblings:small rna processing.description: The maturation of small nucleolar RNAs from host gene introns or independent polycistronic transcripts.

siblings:small rna processing.name: rsRNA Biogenesis
siblings:small rna processing.description: The generation of regulatory small RNAs derived from the processing of ribosomal RNA transcripts.

siblings:small rna processing.name: snRNA Processing
siblings:small rna processing.description: The assembly and maturation steps of small nuclear RNAs required for the formation of the spliceosome.

siblings:small rna processing.name: vRNA Processing
siblings:small rna processing.description: The processing of vault RNAs into small vault RNAs that regulate gene expression and cell signaling.

siblings:snrnp biogenesis.name: U7 snRNP Biogenesis
siblings:snrnp biogenesis.description: The formation of the U7 small nuclear ribonucleoprotein complex involved in the 3-prime end processing of histone pre-mRNAs.

siblings:snrnp biogenesis.name: Export of snRNA
siblings:snrnp biogenesis.description: The mediated transport of newly transcribed small nuclear RNAs from the nucleus to the cytoplasm for further maturation.

siblings:snrnp biogenesis.name: Nuclear Import of snRNPs
siblings:snrnp biogenesis.description: The active transport of assembled snRNP particles from the cytoplasm back into the nucleus to perform splicing functions.

siblings:snrnp biogenesis.name: Assembly of the Sm core
siblings:snrnp biogenesis.description: The coordinated loading of Sm proteins onto the conserved Sm-binding site of snRNAs, a critical step in snRNP maturation.

siblings:snrnp biogenesis.name: Minor spliceosomal snRNP assembly
siblings:snrnp biogenesis.description: The specific biogenesis pathways for snRNPs such as U11, U12, U4atac, and U6atac involved in the minor spliceosome.

siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.name: MAPK Signaling Pathway
siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.description: Consists of p38, JNK, and ERK cascades that mediate cellular responses to external stress and pro-inflammatory cytokines.

siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.name: JAK-STAT Signaling Pathway
siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.description: Transmits signals from extracellular chemical signals, such as interleukins and interferons, directly to the nucleus for gene transcription.

siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.name: Toll-like Receptor (TLR) Signaling
siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.description: A primary innate immune pathway that recognizes pathogen-associated molecular patterns to initiate the inflammatory response.

siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.name: TNF-alpha Signaling Pathway
siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.description: Activated by Tumor Necrosis Factor alpha to regulate systemic inflammation and programmed cell death.

siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.name: Inflammasome Signaling
siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.description: A multiprotein complex pathway that activates caspase-1, leading to the maturation and secretion of IL-1beta and IL-18.

siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.name: AP-1 Signaling Pathway
siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.description: A transcription factor-driven pathway that works in conjunction with NF-kappaB to regulate the expression of inflammatory mediators.

siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.name: cGAS-STING Signaling Pathway
siblings:pro-inflammatory signaling.description: Detects cytosolic DNA as a danger signal to induce type I interferons and other pro-inflammatory cytokines.

siblings:pre-mrna processing.name: 5' Capping
siblings:pre-mrna processing.description: The addition of a 7-methylguanosine cap to the 5' end of the nascent pre-mRNA transcript.

siblings:pre-mrna processing.name: 3' Cleavage and Polyadenylation
siblings:pre-mrna processing.description: The enzymatic cleavage of the pre-mRNA transcript followed by the addition of a polyadenosine tail.

siblings:pre-mrna processing.name: RNA Editing
siblings:pre-mrna processing.description: The process of site-specific alteration of RNA sequences, such as A-to-I or C-to-U conversions.

siblings:pre-mrna processing.name: mRNA Export from Nucleus
siblings:pre-mrna processing.description: The translocation of processed mRNA through the nuclear pore complex into the cytoplasm.

siblings:pre-mrna processing.name: N6-methyladenosine (m6A) Methylation
siblings:pre-mrna processing.description: A prevalent internal chemical modification of the pre-mRNA that influences stability and translation.

siblings:pre-mrna processing.name: Transcription Termination
siblings:pre-mrna processing.description: The process by which RNA polymerase stops transcribing and releases the pre-mRNA molecule.

siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.name: Hemidesmosome Assembly
siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.description: Specialized adhesion complexes linking intermediate filaments to the basement membrane.

siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.name: Syndecan-Mediated Signaling
siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.description: Interactions involving heparan sulfate proteoglycans that modulate cell-matrix binding.

siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.name: CD44-Hyaluronan Interaction
siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.description: Cell surface receptor binding to hyaluronan-rich extracellular matrices.

siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.name: Dystroglycan-Mediated Adhesion
siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.description: Transmembrane linkage between laminin in the basement membrane and the cytoskeleton.

siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.name: Discoidin Domain Receptor Signaling
siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.description: Collagen-activated receptor tyrosine kinases that coordinate cell attachment and matrix remodeling.

siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.name: Tenascin-Mediated Adhesion
siblings:cell-matrix adhesion.description: Cellular interactions with tenascins, large oligomeric glycoproteins of the extracellular matrix.

siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.name: Ribosome Biogenesis
siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.description: The multi-step process of synthesizing and assembling ribosomal RNA and proteins into functional ribosomes.

siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.name: snRNP Assembly
siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.description: The pathway responsible for forming small nuclear ribonucleoprotein particles required for pre-mRNA splicing.

siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.name: snoRNP Assembly
siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.description: The assembly of small nucleolar ribonucleoproteins involved in the modification and processing of ribosomal RNA.

siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.name: Telomerase Holoenzyme Assembly
siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.description: The coordinated formation of the telomerase complex consisting of telomerase reverse transcriptase and telomerase RNA.

siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.name: Signal Recognition Particle Assembly
siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.description: The biogenesis of the RNP complex that mediates the targeting of nascent proteins to the endoplasmic reticulum.

siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.name: Ribonucleoprotein Complex Localization
siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.description: The biological process of transporting and positioning ribonucleoprotein complexes to their functional cellular sites.

siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.name: RNA-Protein Complex Subunit Organization
siblings:ribonucleoprotein complex bioge.description: The process resulting in the assembly, arrangement, or disassembly of the protein and RNA subunits of an RNP complex.

siblings:cellular component biogenesis.name: Mitochondrion Biogenesis
siblings:cellular component biogenesis.description: The cellular process resulting in the formation, organization, or maintenance of a mitochondrion.

siblings:cellular component biogenesis.name: Cell Wall Biogenesis
siblings:cellular component biogenesis.description: The synthesis and assembly of the structural components that form the cell wall.

siblings:cellular component biogenesis.name: Membrane Biogenesis
