siblings:cellular component biogenesis.description: The process of forming new biological membranes through the synthesis and assembly of lipids and proteins.

siblings:cellular component biogenesis.name: Cilium Assembly
siblings:cellular component biogenesis.description: The formation and structural organization of a cilium, including the assembly of the microtubule axoneme.

siblings:cellular component biogenesis.name: Cytoskeleton Organization
siblings:cellular component biogenesis.description: The assembly, disassembly, or arrangement of the cytoskeleton components such as microtubules and actin filaments.

siblings:cellular component biogenesis.name: Protein Complex Assembly
siblings:cellular component biogenesis.description: The process of aggregating and arranging multiple protein subunits to form a stable, functional complex.

siblings:mitochondrial quality control.name: Mitophagy
siblings:mitochondrial quality control.description: The selective degradation of damaged or redundant mitochondria through the autophagic pathway to prevent cellular dysfunction.

siblings:mitochondrial quality control.name: Mitochondrial Biogenesis
siblings:mitochondrial quality control.description: The process of generating new mitochondria to maintain or increase mitochondrial mass and metabolic capacity.

siblings:mitochondrial quality control.name: Mitochondrial Fusion
siblings:mitochondrial quality control.description: The merging of two mitochondria to exchange proteins, lipids, and mtDNA, helping to buffer local damage.

siblings:mitochondrial quality control.name: Mitochondrial Fission
siblings:mitochondrial quality control.description: The division of a mitochondrion into smaller units, facilitating organelle distribution and the isolation of damaged segments.

siblings:mitochondrial quality control.name: Mitochondrial Proteostasis
siblings:mitochondrial quality control.description: The maintenance of the mitochondrial proteome via chaperones, proteases, and the mitochondrial unfolded protein response (UPRmt).

siblings:mitochondrial quality control.name: Mitochondrial-derived Vesicles (MDVs)
siblings:mitochondrial quality control.description: Small vesicles that transport cargo from the mitochondria to lysosomes or peroxisomes for the targeted removal of damaged proteins.

siblings:rna splicing.name: Major Pathway of RNA Splicing
siblings:rna splicing.description: The primary mechanism for removing U2-type introns from pre-mRNA using the major spliceosome.

siblings:rna splicing.name: Minor Pathway of RNA Splicing
siblings:rna splicing.description: The mechanism for removing rare U12-type introns using a specialized minor spliceosome.

siblings:rna splicing.name: Alternative Splicing
siblings:rna splicing.description: The regulated process of selecting different splice sites to produce multiple protein isoforms from one gene.

siblings:rna splicing.name: Exon Junction Complex (EJC) Assembly
siblings:rna splicing.description: The deposition of protein complexes at splice junctions to assist in mRNA transport and surveillance.

siblings:rna splicing.name: Lariat Removal and Degradation
siblings:rna splicing.description: The enzymatic debranching and breakdown of excised introns to recycle nucleotides and factors.

siblings:rna splicing.name: Spliceosome Disassembly and Recycling
siblings:rna splicing.description: The process of releasing components from the post-catalytic spliceosome for use in future splicing events.

siblings:post-translational protein modi.name: Phosphorylation
siblings:post-translational protein modi.description: The addition of a phosphate group to a protein, typically on serine, threonine, or tyrosine residues, to regulate activity.

siblings:post-translational protein modi.name: Glycosylation
siblings:post-translational protein modi.description: The attachment of carbohydrate chains to proteins, essential for protein folding, stability, and cell-cell recognition.

siblings:post-translational protein modi.name: Acetylation
siblings:post-translational protein modi.description: The addition of an acetyl group, often occurring on lysine residues of histones to regulate gene expression.

siblings:post-translational protein modi.name: Methylation
siblings:post-translational protein modi.description: The transfer of methyl groups to amino acids like lysine or arginine, frequently involved in epigenetic regulation.

siblings:post-translational protein modi.name: Lipidation
siblings:post-translational protein modi.description: The covalent attachment of lipid groups, such as myristoyl or palmitoyl groups, to facilitate membrane anchoring.

siblings:post-translational protein modi.name: SUMOylation
siblings:post-translational protein modi.description: The covalent attachment of Small Ubiquitin-like Modifier (SUMO) proteins to regulate nuclear-cytosolic transport and transcriptional activity.

siblings:post-translational protein modi.name: Proteolysis
siblings:post-translational protein modi.description: The targeted cleavage of peptide bonds by proteases to activate, deactivate, or degrade proteins.

siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.name: Receptor Serine/Threonine Kinase Signaling
siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.description: Signaling via receptors with intrinsic serine/threonine kinase activity, primarily utilized by the TGF-beta superfamily.

siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.name: Tyrosine Kinase-Associated Receptors
siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.description: Receptors lacking intrinsic enzymatic activity that signal by recruiting and activating non-covalently bound cytoplasmic tyrosine kinases like JAK.

siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.name: Receptor Guanylyl Cyclases
siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.description: Receptors that catalyze the conversion of GTP to the second messenger cGMP upon extracellular ligand binding.

siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.name: Receptor Tyrosine Phosphatases
siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.description: Receptors that act by removing phosphate groups from tyrosine residues on specific intracellular signaling proteins.

siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.name: Histidine-Kinase-Associated Receptors
siblings:enzyme-linked receptor signalin.description: Receptors that signal through a mechanism involving the autophosphorylation of a histidine residue, commonly found in bacterial chemotaxis.

siblings:transcription.name: RNA Polymerase I Transcription
siblings:transcription.description: The process of synthesizing ribosomal RNA precursors by RNA polymerase I.

siblings:transcription.name: RNA Polymerase II Transcription
siblings:transcription.description: The synthesis of messenger RNA and various non-coding RNAs by RNA polymerase II.

siblings:transcription.name: RNA Polymerase III Transcription
siblings:transcription.description: The synthesis of small RNAs like transfer RNA and 5S ribosomal RNA by RNA polymerase III.

siblings:transcription.name: Transcription Initiation
siblings:transcription.description: The assembly of the transcription machinery at the promoter region of a gene.

siblings:transcription.name: Transcription Elongation
siblings:transcription.description: The phase where RNA polymerase synthesizes a complementary RNA strand from the DNA template.

siblings:transcription.name: Transcription Termination
siblings:transcription.description: The phase where the RNA polymerase stops transcription and releases the newly synthesized RNA.

siblings:transcription.name: Mitochondrial Transcription
siblings:transcription.description: The expression of genes encoded within the mitochondrial genome.

siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.name: Ubiquitin Activation
siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.description: The initial ATP-dependent step where ubiquitin is activated by an E1 enzyme via the formation of a high-energy thioester bond.

siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.name: Ubiquitin Conjugation
siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.description: The transfer of activated ubiquitin from an E1 enzyme to an E2 ubiquitin-conjugating enzyme.

siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.name: Ubiquitin Ligation
siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.description: The final step of the cascade where an E3 ligase facilitates the covalent attachment of ubiquitin to a lysine residue on the target substrate.

siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.name: Monoubiquitination
siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.description: The addition of a single ubiquitin molecule to a substrate, typically regulating processes such as endocytosis, histone regulation, and nuclear transport.

siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.name: K48-linked Polyubiquitination
siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.description: The formation of ubiquitin chains linked via Lysine 48, which serves as the primary signal for targeting proteins to the 26S proteasome for degradation.

siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.name: K63-linked Polyubiquitination
siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.description: The assembly of ubiquitin chains linked via Lysine 63, involved in non-proteolytic signaling, DNA damage repair, and inflammatory responses.

siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.name: Linear Ubiquitination
siblings:ubiquitin-mediated protein modi.description: The head-to-tail assembly of ubiquitin chains linked via the N-terminal methionine (M1), predominantly involved in the regulation of NF-kappaB signaling.

siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.name: G Protein-Coupled Receptor Signaling
siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.description: Transduction of signals through seven-transmembrane receptors that activate heterotrimeric G proteins.

siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.name: Ion Channel-Linked Receptor Signaling
siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.description: Signaling where ligand binding induces a conformational change that opens or closes a transmembrane ion pore.

siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.name: Cytokine Receptor Signaling
siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.description: Signaling through receptors that lack intrinsic enzymatic activity and rely on recruited kinases like JAKs.

siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.name: Integrin Signaling
siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.description: Mechanotransduction and chemical signaling resulting from cell adhesion to the extracellular matrix.

siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.name: Notch Signaling
siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.description: A juxtacrine signaling mechanism involving the proteolytic cleavage of the Notch receptor upon contact with adjacent cell ligands.

siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.name: Wnt Signaling
siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.description: Pathway regulating cell fate and development through the stabilization of beta-catenin or calcium release.

siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.name: Hedgehog Signaling
siblings:cell surface receptor signaling.description: A pathway essential for embryonic development that regulates gene expression via the Gli family of transcription factors.

siblings:cell adhesion.name: Cell-Cell Adhesion
siblings:cell adhesion.description: The interaction and attachment of a cell to another cell via cell-surface adhesion molecules like cadherins.

siblings:cell adhesion.name: Adherens Junctions
siblings:cell adhesion.description: Protein complexes that link the actin cytoskeletons of adjacent cells to provide structural cohesion.

siblings:cell adhesion.name: Tight Junctions
siblings:cell adhesion.description: Specialized connections that seal adjacent cells together to form a barrier that limits the passage of molecules.

siblings:cell adhesion.name: Desmosomes
siblings:cell adhesion.description: Junctional complexes that join intermediate filaments of neighboring cells, providing high tensile strength.

siblings:cell adhesion.name: Gap Junctions
siblings:cell adhesion.description: Intercellular channels that permit the direct transfer of ions and small signaling molecules between cells.

siblings:cell adhesion.name: Leukocyte-Endothelial Adhesion
siblings:cell adhesion.description: The process involving selectins and integrins that allows white blood cells to bind to the vascular wall during inflammation.

siblings:transmembrane transport.name: Protein Translocation
siblings:transmembrane transport.description: The movement of proteins across or into a biological membrane, often occurring co-translationally or post-translationally.

siblings:transmembrane transport.name: RNA Transport
siblings:transmembrane transport.description: The directed movement of various RNA species across membranes, most notably across the nuclear envelope via the nuclear pore complex.

siblings:transmembrane transport.name: Vesicle-mediated Transport
siblings:transmembrane transport.description: The transport of molecules and substances between membrane-bound compartments using transport vesicles.

siblings:transmembrane transport.name: Ion Transport
siblings:transmembrane transport.description: The regulated movement of ions through specialized transmembrane channels, carriers, or active pumps to maintain electrochemical gradients.

siblings:transmembrane transport.name: Macromolecule Transport
siblings:transmembrane transport.description: The process of moving large molecules, such as complex polysaccharides or lipid complexes, across membrane barriers.

siblings:transmembrane transport.name: Subcellular Organelle Transport
siblings:transmembrane transport.description: The movement of solutes and molecules across the specific membranes of organelles like mitochondria, peroxisomes, or the endoplasmic reticulum.

siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).name: Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor (EGFR) Signaling
siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).description: Regulates cell growth, proliferation, and survival through the binding of EGF-family ligands.

siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).name: Fibroblast Growth Factor Receptor (FGFR) Signaling
siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).description: Essential for embryonic development, angiogenesis, and wound healing processes.

siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).name: Insulin Receptor Signaling
siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).description: The primary mediator of glucose homeostasis and metabolic regulation in response to insulin.

siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).name: Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor Receptor (VEGFR) Signaling
siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).description: A critical pathway for the stimulation of angiogenesis and vascular permeability.

siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).name: Platelet-Derived Growth Factor Receptor (PDGFR) Signaling
siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).description: Involved in the regulation of cell migration, proliferation, and survival, particularly in mesenchymal cells.

siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).name: Trk Receptor Signaling
siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).description: Activated by neurotrophins to promote neuronal survival, development, and synaptic plasticity.

siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).name: MET Signaling
siblings:receptor tyrosine kinase (rtk).description: Mediated by hepatocyte growth factor to regulate cell motility, invasive growth, and tissue regeneration.

siblings:extracellular signal transducti.name: Intracellular Receptor Signaling
siblings:extracellular signal transducti.description: Transduction involving lipophilic signaling molecules that diffuse across the cell membrane to bind receptors located in the cytoplasm or nucleus.

siblings:extracellular signal transducti.name: Gap Junction Signaling
siblings:extracellular signal transducti.description: The direct transmission of ions and small signaling molecules between adjacent cells through specialized protein channels known as connexons.

siblings:extracellular signal transducti.name: Extracellular Matrix Mediated Signaling
siblings:extracellular signal transducti.description: Signaling initiated by the physical and chemical interaction of a cell with its surrounding matrix, typically via integrin receptors.

siblings:extracellular signal transducti.name: Juxtacrine Signaling
siblings:extracellular signal transducti.description: A contact-dependent signaling mechanism where membrane-bound ligands on the surface of one cell interact directly with receptors on a neighboring cell.

siblings:extracellular signal transducti.name: Exosome-mediated Signaling
siblings:extracellular signal transducti.description: Communication involving the secretion and uptake of extracellular vesicles containing proteins, lipids, and various RNA species.
